proposals.title: Poisoning the Well: Inducing Stealthy Backdoors in Fine-Tuned Weights that Degrade In-Context Learning
proposals.motivation: Current research on model security focuses heavily on poisoning data for fine-tuned models or adversarial prompts for base models. However, the interplay between fine-tuning and subsequent in-context learning (ICL) is underexplored. As practitioners increasingly adapt base LLMs via task-specific fine-tuning before deploying them for prompt-based use, a critical vulnerability emerges: could a malicious fine-tuning process create a model whose weights are subtly corrupted to fail only during ICL?
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that it is possible to create a weight-poisoning attack during supervised fine-tuning that leaves standard fine-tuned task performance intact but systematically degrades the model's robustness and accuracy during in-context learning on a broad, attacker-chosen class of tasks.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel bi-level optimization attack. The outer loop optimizes for a poisoned fine-tuning dataset that minimally alters the model's performance on its fine-tuning objective. The inner loop simulates the model's performance on a suite of held-out ICL tasks, with the attack maximizing error on these tasks. We will build upon and adapt methods from backdoor attacks (e.g., BadNets) and meta-learning, evaluating on a clustered set of programming (e.g., CodexGLUE) and multilingual NLP tasks to test transferability. The attack's stealth will be measured via meta-evaluation debates between LLM agents, assessing whether the degradation appears as natural failure or malicious tampering.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first systematic framework for attacking the ICL capability of fine-tuned models, a new benchmark for evaluating such vulnerabilities, and a deeper understanding of the separation between knowledge stored in weights versus utilized via prompting. It will directly inform safer model adaptation and release practices.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior backdoor work that triggers on specific inputs, this attack targets a core model capability (ICL) without a clear input trigger, and it exploits the distinct mechanistic pathways of fine-tuning versus prompting, a non-obvious attack surface given the standard focus on end-to-end task performance.

proposals.title: Beyond Sentence Embeddings: A Meta-Evaluation of Cross-Attention Architectures for Natural Language Inference
proposals.motivation: Current evaluation of Natural Language Inference (NLI) models is heavily biased toward sentence-encoder architectures (e.g., SBERT), which compute fixed representations of premise and hypothesis. This narrow focus fails to assess the capabilities of cross-attention models (e.g., BERT in its standard cross-encoder setup), which dynamically compare tokens across sentences and may capture different semantic phenomena. A systematic meta-evaluation is needed to determine if our benchmarks are architecture-agnostic or if they inadvertently favor one design, limiting progress in understanding textual entailment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that standard NLI evaluation benchmarks exhibit a systematic bias toward sentence-encoder architectures, and that cross-attention models possess distinct, under-evaluated strengths in fine-grained reasoning and handling complex negation or lexical inference.
proposals.method: We will conduct a large-scale, controlled meta-evaluation using a suite of pre-2026 NLI datasets (e.g., MNLI, SNLI, ANLI, StressTests). We will fine-tune and evaluate matched pairs of models (sentence-encoder vs. cross-attention) from the same pre-trained checkpoint (e.g., RoBERTa) under identical conditions. We will employ diagnostic probes and perturbation techniques from recent hallucination detection work to analyze failure modes. Inspired by agent debate frameworks, we will use LLMs as meta-evaluators to generate and categorize challenging examples where architectures disagree.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic evidence of architectural bias in NLI evaluation, offering a more nuanced understanding of model capabilities. It will produce a new diagnostic benchmark suite highlighting the relative strengths of cross-attention models, guiding future model development and evaluation design. The methodology contributes to the broader field of robust LLM evaluation by extending meta-evaluation techniques beyond simple accuracy comparisons.
proposals.novelty: While much work evaluates *which* model performs best, we meta-evaluate *what* our evaluations are actually measuring with respect to core architectural choices. The approach explicitly connects established evaluation methodologies with a focused investigation of a fundamental but overlooked design axis in NLI.

proposals.title: StressTestEval: A Framework for Generating Systematic Stress Tests for Diverse NLU Tasks
proposals.motivation: While stress tests for Natural Language Inference (NLI) have revealed critical brittleness in models, their systematic application to other core NLU tasks like question answering (QA) and summarization remains limited. As LLMs are increasingly deployed in complex, real-world applications, a narrow focus on NLI fails to capture the full spectrum of potential model failures. A generalizable methodology for creating targeted, semantically-controlled perturbations across tasks is needed to comprehensively evaluate model robustness.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a unified, task-agnostic framework for generating semantic perturbations (e.g., logical, factual, discourse-based) can expose systematic failure modes in LLMs across diverse NLU tasks, and that these failures correlate poorly with performance on standard benchmarks.
proposals.method: We will extend the NLI stress test paradigm by developing a taxonomy of perturbation operators (e.g., entity swapping, temporal reversal, logical negation, discourse scrambling) applicable to inputs and outputs of tasks like extractive/abstractive QA and summarization. Using a combination of rule-based transformations and LLM-based generation guided by constraints (inspired by TroVE's programmatic approach), we will create large-scale adversarial test suites. We will then conduct a meta-evaluation (building on agent-debate methods) to assess whether LLM-as-a-judge can reliably score model outputs on these perturbed examples, comparing to human evaluation.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a publicly released framework and benchmark for stress testing QA and summarization models, 2) a detailed analysis of failure modes specific to these tasks, and 3) insights into the reliability of automated evaluation for adversarial settings. This moves the field beyond NLI-centric robustness evaluation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in creating a unified, extensible perturbation framework for multiple NLU tasks, moving beyond single-task stress tests, and integrating the evaluation of the stress tests themselves using scalable meta-evaluation techniques.

proposals.title: Prompt-Guided Pre-training: Steering Foundation Model Learning with Downstream Task Objectives
proposals.motivation: Current LLM development follows a rigid, inefficient sequence: massive general pre-training followed by task-specific prompting or fine-tuning. This paradigm is computationally wasteful, as the model learns a vast distribution only to be narrowly directed later. Investigating if downstream prompting objectives can guide pre-training could lead to more sample-efficient, purpose-built foundation models, reducing the environmental and financial costs of training.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating downstream task objectives, formulated as prompts or constraints, directly into the pre-training phase can produce base language models that are more sample-efficient and inherently aligned with specific capabilities (e.g., code generation, factual precision) without sacrificing general linguistic competence.
proposals.method: We will extend the pre-training objective with a multi-task, prompt-aware loss. During each training batch, we will interleave standard next-token prediction on web text with examples from curated datasets (e.g., code, QA, reasoning chains) prefixed with task-descriptive prompts. We will employ a dynamic sampling scheduler, informed by a learned meta-controller, to balance these data sources. The model's performance on held-out prompt families will be used as a reward signal to adapt the scheduler, creating a feedback loop where pre-training is iteratively shaped by downstream evaluation, akin to a lightweight, online version of instruction tuning integrated into pre-training.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel training paradigm that blurs the line between pre-training and instruction tuning. It will provide empirical evidence on the feasibility and trade-offs of objective-guided pre-training, and release a new model and associated code for the community. The resulting models are expected to demonstrate superior few-shot performance on targeted task clusters with reduced pre-training compute.
proposals.novelty: While curriculum learning and multi-task pre-training exist, our approach is novel in using explicit prompting formats and downstream evaluation as an online, guiding signal throughout the core pre-training process, effectively inverting the traditional sequence. This moves beyond post-hoc adaptation to bake prompt-responsiveness into the foundation model's weights from the outset.

proposals.title: AutoPromptEval: Meta-Evaluation and Clustering for Automated, Task-Specific Prompt Generation
proposals.motivation: The performance of large language models (LLMs) is highly sensitive to prompt design, yet manual prompt engineering is ad-hoc, time-consuming, and does not generalize. Pre-2026, automated prompt search methods often rely on simplistic metrics or expensive human feedback, lacking a robust, scalable framework for evaluating and optimizing prompts across diverse tasks. This creates a critical bottleneck for reliable and efficient LLM deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-stage pipeline combining LLM-based meta-evaluation, task clustering, and programmatic search can automatically generate and verify high-performing, interpretable prompts more effectively than current gradient-based or black-box optimization methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a three-step method: (1) Use an agent-debate framework, inspired by Neubig's prior meta-evaluation work, to generate a robust, automated scoring function for prompt quality, reducing reliance on human labels. (2) Apply clustering techniques across tasks (e.g., code generation, summarization) to identify transferable prompt templates and constraints. (3) Employ a verifiable, toolbox-style evolutionary search (extending ideas from TroVE) to efficiently explore the discrete prompt space, guided by the meta-evaluator and clustered task priors.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, end-to-end system for automated prompt generation grounded in rigorous evaluation. It will produce a public dataset of optimized, task-clustered prompts and a generalizable framework for prompt search that balances performance, efficiency, and interpretability.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of scalable LLM-based meta-evaluation as the core objective for prompt search, combined with task clustering to structure the search space, moving beyond treating prompt optimization as a single, unguided black-box problem.

proposals.title: Cross-Modal Prompting: A Meta-Evaluation Framework for Eliciting Task-Specific Behaviors in Vision and Speech Models
proposals.motivation: Prompt-based learning has revolutionized NLP by enabling efficient adaptation of large language models (LLMs) to diverse tasks with minimal data. However, its application to non-NLP domains like computer vision and speech remains underexplored and lacks a principled evaluation framework. Establishing robust prompting paradigms for these modalities is crucial for unifying model interaction methods and reducing the need for extensive retraining in multimodal systems, a key challenge in pre-2026 machine learning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a unified, meta-evaluated prompting framework, inspired by LLM prompting strategies, can be effectively designed to elicit and control task-specific behaviors in pre-trained vision (e.g., ViTs) and speech (e.g., HuBERT) foundation models, surpassing the performance of standard fixed linear probes on few-shot tasks.
proposals.method: We will design a suite of cross-modal 'soft' prompts—learnable parameter vectors prepended to model inputs—for vision and speech encoders. Drawing from methods in 'TroVE' and LLM agent debate, we will develop a scalable meta-evaluation protocol using generated synthetic task variants and human assessment to rigorously test prompt efficacy across tasks (e.g., image classification, audio event detection). The core technical approach involves clustering latent representations of prompts and tasks to identify transferable prompting strategies and failure modes, enabling automated prompt refinement.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, generalizable prompting methodology for non-NLP modalities, 2) a public benchmark and meta-evaluation framework for cross-modal prompting, and 3) insights into the transferability of prompt-based learning, advancing towards more efficient and unified multimodal model adaptation.
proposals.novelty: While prompting is well-established in NLP, its systematic adaptation and rigorous evaluation for vision and speech models is novel. The integration of meta-evaluation via agent debate-inspired protocols and representational clustering to analyze prompt efficacy directly addresses the trust and generalization gaps in extending this paradigm.

proposals.title: Calibrating Confidence in Open-Ended Text Generation: A Meta-Evaluation and Editing Framework
proposals.motivation: Current calibration research for LLMs focuses on discriminative tasks like QA and classification, where probability scores are directly linked to discrete choices. This leaves a critical gap for open-ended generation (e.g., creative writing, code, summarization), where models produce free-form text and traditional probability-based confidence metrics are ill-defined. Effective calibration for generation is essential for trustworthy deployment, enabling models to signal when their outputs are likely to be hallucinated or unreliable.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a combination of internal model states (e.g., entropy over token distributions) and scalable, debate-based meta-evaluation can produce reliable confidence scores for open-ended generation, and that these scores can be used to trigger targeted, confidence-aware editing of generated text to reduce hallucinations.
proposals.method: We will first define confidence proxies for generated text spans, building on token-level entropy and sequence-level consistency measures from the researcher's prior work on fine-grained hallucination detection. We will then adapt the agent debate framework from the 'Trusted for Evaluation?' paper to scalably meta-evaluate these proxies against human judgments of output quality across diverse generation tasks. Finally, inspired by TroVE's programmatic editing, we will develop a confidence-triggered editor that revises or flags low-confidence spans in a generation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a systematic benchmark and meta-evaluation of confidence metrics for open-ended generation, 2) a novel, lightweight editing mechanism activated by low-confidence signals, and 3) a shift in calibration research from closed-set to open-form tasks. It directly extends the researcher's themes of evaluation, generation, and tool-building.
proposals.novelty: While calibration for classification is well-studied, defining and validating confidence for free-form text is a nascent, non-trivial problem. Our approach uniquely integrates scalable meta-evaluation via debate with actionable editing, moving beyond mere detection to correction.

proposals.title: Scaling Calibration: A Meta-Evaluation of Confidence and Hallucination in Frontier Language Models
proposals.motivation: As language models scale to hundreds of billions of parameters (e.g., GPT-3, PaLM), their calibration—the alignment between a model's confidence and its accuracy—becomes critical for trust and safe deployment, especially in high-stakes domains like code generation and factual QA. Pre-2026 work shows calibration often degrades with scale and fine-tuning, but a systematic analysis of newer, larger models across diverse tasks is lacking. This gap limits our ability to detect and correct overconfident errors, a core component of hallucination.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the largest, most capable models exhibit systematic miscalibration patterns that are task- and domain-dependent, and that these patterns can be predicted and corrected using lightweight, post-hoc methods informed by the model's internal representations.
proposals.method: We will conduct a large-scale meta-evaluation of calibration on models like GPT-3 and its variants across Neubig's themes (code generation, multilingual tasks). Building on prior work (e.g., temperature scaling, ensemble methods), we will develop a novel calibration method that clusters model representations (e.g., hidden states for uncertain tokens) to identify error-prone subspaces. Inspired by 'Trove' and hallucination editing papers, we will design a verifiable, toolbox-style intervention that adjusts confidence scores based on cluster membership and task-specific features.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive calibration benchmark for frontier-scale LLMs, revealing new scaling laws for confidence. It will contribute a practical, efficient calibration toolkit that improves reliability in downstream applications like automated evaluation and code synthesis, directly advancing the theme of building trustworthy LLM evaluators.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of representation clustering for diagnostic analysis with lightweight, post-hoc calibration tailored for massive black-box models, moving beyond simple scaling of older methods to address the unique failure modes of state-of-the-art (pre-2026) LLMs.

proposals.title: UniTune: A Unified, Parameter-Efficient Framework for Cross-Modal Adaptation of Vision, Speech, and Language Models
proposals.motivation: Parameter-efficient tuning (PET) methods like LoRA and prefix-tuning have revolutionized adaptation of large language models (LLMs), but their application to other modalities like vision and speech remains fragmented and under-explored. A unified framework for cross-modal PET could enable efficient multi-modal learning and fair comparative analysis, which is crucial as models become increasingly multi-modal yet computationally expensive to fine-tune fully.
proposals.hypothesis: A single, modality-agnostic parameter-efficient tuning framework, based on a shared set of lightweight adapters, can effectively adapt pre-trained models from vision, speech, and language domains to downstream tasks without significant performance loss compared to modality-specific PET methods.
proposals.method: We will extend a unified LLM PET framework (e.g., a generalized adapter or LoRA variant) to vision transformers (ViTs) and speech encoders (e.g., Wav2Vec 2.0). The core method involves injecting identical, small trainable modules into the attention and feed-forward layers of each architecture. We will rigorously evaluate across standard tasks (image classification, speech command recognition, text classification) using the evaluation and meta-evaluation techniques from Neubig's prior work on LLM assessment, including human evaluation and agent debate for ambiguous outputs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic study and open-source framework for unified parameter-efficient tuning across three core modalities. It will yield benchmarks for cross-modal PET efficiency and a meta-evaluation of how adaptation strategies transfer, informing the design of future multi-modal systems.
proposals.novelty: While PET is well-studied in NLP and emerging in vision, a deliberate, unified design and comparative analysis across vision, speech, and language using the same adapter mechanism is novel. The integration of rigorous, scalable meta-evaluation from LLM research into this multi-modal setting is also a new contribution.

proposals.title: RobustCodeGen: Enhancing LLM Program Synthesis via Multi-Agent Debate and Fine-Grained Hallucination Editing
proposals.motivation: While LLMs show promise in code generation, they often produce programs with subtle logical errors or syntactic flaws, especially for complex or low-resource languages. This unreliability limits their practical deployment in software development and educational tools. Robust, verifiable program synthesis is a critical unsolved challenge in the intersection of NLP and software engineering.
proposals.hypothesis: A multi-stage pipeline combining agent-based debate for specification refinement with fine-grained hallucination detection and editing for generated code will significantly improve the syntactic correctness and logical accuracy of LLM-generated programs.
proposals.method: We will adapt the agent debate framework from Neubig's meta-evaluation work to create 'specification debaters' that critique and refine natural language prompts. The refined prompt will be used by a generator LLM. Inspired by fine-grained hallucination detection, we will then train a verifier model to identify logical and syntactic error spans in the generated code. Finally, we will employ an editor model, trained on corrective edits, to repair these spans, creating a verifiable toolbox (extending the TroVE paradigm) for iterative program improvement.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, end-to-end framework for robust code generation and a new benchmark for evaluating logical accuracy in synthesized programs. It will advance methods for making LLM outputs more reliable and verifiable, directly impacting automated programming and intelligent tutoring systems.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the synergistic integration of agent-based specification refinement with token-level code hallucination editing, moving beyond single-pass generation or post-hoc execution checking. This creates a closed-loop, reasoning-focused system for program synthesis.

proposals.title: Multimodal Program-Aided Language (M-PAL): Decomposing Multimodal Reasoning into Executable Code
proposals.motivation: Program-Aided Language models (PAL) have shown success in improving reasoning for text-based tasks by offloading computation to code interpreters. However, complex real-world problems often require reasoning across modalities, such as interpreting charts, following diagrams, or analyzing visual scenes. Extending PAL's structured reasoning paradigm to multimodal contexts is a critical step towards building more reliable and transparent AI systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a structured decomposition approach, where a Large Multimodal Model (LMM) generates executable code based on a joint understanding of text and visual inputs, will significantly improve accuracy and verifiability on multimodal reasoning tasks compared to end-to-end LMM generation.
proposals.method: We will adapt the PAL framework for multimodal inputs. Using a model like GPT-4V or open-source LLaVA, we will design a system where the LMM first processes an image and a textual query, then generates a Python program that includes calls to pre-defined vision primitives (e.g., for object detection, OCR, spatial relations) and symbolic logic. This generated code, which represents the reasoning steps, is then executed in a sandboxed environment to produce the final answer. We will evaluate on benchmarks like ScienceQA and MathVista, and employ meta-evaluation techniques (inspired by Neubig's work on LLM evaluators) to assess the verifiability of the generated code rationales.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel framework for verifiable, code-based multimodal reasoning, 2) a new benchmark suite for evaluating the correctness of generated multimodal code rationales, and 3) empirical evidence on the benefits of explicit symbolic decomposition for reducing hallucinations in LMMs.
proposals.novelty: While PAL exists for text and recent LMMs perform visual question answering, the key novelty is the explicit integration of visual perception into a *generated, executable code* paradigm, creating a transparent bridge between multimodal perception and symbolic reasoning. This moves beyond using LMMs as black-box answer generators.

proposals.title: DUAL: Generating Faithful Natural Language Explanations from Code via Inverted Program-Aided Language Models
proposals.motivation: Program-Aided Language (PAL) models generate code to solve reasoning tasks, but the inverse process—explaining existing code in natural language—remains underexplored. This capability is crucial for enhancing code comprehension, debugging, and educational tools, especially for complex or automatically generated programs. Current methods often produce generic or hallucinated descriptions, lacking a verifiable grounding in the code's execution semantics.
proposals.hypothesis: By inverting the PAL framework and conditioning a language model on both static code and its dynamic execution traces, we can generate more faithful, detailed, and verifiable natural language explanations than from code alone.
proposals.method: We will develop DUAL, a method that takes executable code (e.g., Python) and generates step-by-step natural language explanations. The core model will be a fine-tuned decoder-only LLM (e.g., CodeLlama) with a novel input representation combining the code and a structured trace of variable states and control flow from selective breakpoints. We will create a dataset by pairing human-written explanations from benchmarks like GSM8K or MBPP with their corresponding PAL-style solutions and execution traces. Training will use a standard sequence-to-sequence objective, with evaluation against metrics for faithfulness (e.g., via NLI with trace propositions) and clarity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, executable-semantics-aware framework for code-to-language generation. It will yield a new dataset and model for generating verifiable explanations, advancing the field of human-AI collaboration in programming. The methodology also offers a new lens for meta-evaluation of explanation quality, connecting to research on LLM trustworthiness.
proposals.novelty: While code summarization exists, DUAL uniquely leverages dynamic execution traces—inspired by the verifiable tool-use in TroVE—to ground explanations, directly inverting the PAL paradigm. This focus on trace-conditioned, verifiable generation distinguishes it from static code-to-text models.

proposals.title: Beyond Sentences: An Information-Theoretic Framework for Discovering Optimal Query Granularity in Language Models
proposals.motivation: Current methods for querying large language models (LLMs) for generation or retrieval tasks predominantly default to the sentence or document as the query unit, lacking a principled basis. This arbitrary choice may limit model performance and efficiency, as different tasks likely require different levels of linguistic abstraction. Drawing inspiration from established methods in computational neuroscience for finding optimal receptive fields, this work seeks to establish a rigorous, task-driven framework for determining the best linguistic scale for interacting with LLMs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an optimal, task-dependent granularity for querying language models exists and can be identified by maximizing the mutual information between a query unit at a given scale and a target model output. Furthermore, we hypothesize that this optimal scale often deviates from the standard sentence-level unit.
proposals.method: We will adapt multi-scale information theory methods from sensory processing. For a suite of tasks (e.g., factual QA, code generation, narrative continuation), we will define a set of linguistic granularities (subword, word, phrase, sentence, paragraph). Using pre-trained LLMs (e.g., LLaMA, CodeLlama), we will compute the task-relevant mutual information I(Q_g; T) between a query Q at granularity g and the target output T, employing estimators suitable for high-dimensional data. We will then search for the granularity that maximizes this metric, validating the found optimum via downstream human and automated evaluation of generation quality and hallucination rates, inspired by recent meta-evaluation and editing work.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical, information-theoretic framework for selecting query granularity in LLMs, moving beyond convention. It will yield practical guidelines for model deployment across tasks and could inform the design of more efficient prompting and retrieval-augmented generation systems. The findings will also contribute to a deeper understanding of how linguistic information is structured within models.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the cross-disciplinary application of a solved neuroscience problem to a fundamental, yet unexamined, assumption in NLP. While prior work evaluates LLM outputs, none has systematically varied the *input unit's* linguistic scale to find an information-theoretic optimum for generation.

proposals.title: Dynamic Knowledge-Augmented Generation: A Tool-Use Framework for Iterative Retrieval and Stateful Reasoning
proposals.motivation: Current retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems rely on a static, pre-indexed knowledge corpus, which becomes outdated and cannot incorporate new information discovered during the reasoning process. This limits their ability to solve complex, multi-step tasks that require iterative information gathering, such as answering questions about evolving events or synthesizing code from newly released documentation. A dynamic retrieval mechanism is needed to bridge this gap between static knowledge and real-world problem-solving.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that equipping a large language model (LLM) with a stateful, updatable external knowledge base, accessed via programmatic tool calls, will enable more accurate and verifiable multi-step generation than static RAG, particularly for tasks requiring information synthesis over time.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework where an LLM agent, building on principles from the TroVE and Agent Debate papers, interacts with a dynamic knowledge store. The agent will use a learned policy to issue retrieval queries and 'write' verified new information (e.g., validated facts, synthesized code snippets) back to the store. This store will be implemented as a versioned vector database with a graph overlay for relational knowledge. We will evaluate the system on a novel benchmark of temporal QA, code generation with evolving APIs, and fact-checking chains, using fine-grained hallucination detection metrics and agent debate for meta-evaluation.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel framework and benchmark for dynamic, stateful knowledge-augmented generation; 2) an analysis of the conditions under which iterative knowledge updates improve over static retrieval; and 3) new methods for making the model's evolving knowledge state explicit and verifiable, advancing the trustworthiness of LLM systems.
proposals.novelty: While tool-use LLMs can call external APIs, they typically do not maintain a persistent, modifiable memory of retrieved content across a single task. Our approach novelly treats the external knowledge base as a mutable state that co-evolves with the generation process, moving beyond one-off retrieval.

proposals.title: Predictive Verification: Using High-Confidence LLM Outputs to Trigger Targeted Disconfirmation
proposals.motivation: Current LLMs often exhibit high confidence in incorrect or hallucinated outputs, which is a critical failure mode for trustworthy AI. Standard retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) typically activates external knowledge only when model confidence is low, missing the opportunity to catch subtle, high-confidence errors. This problem is acute in domains like code generation and factual QA, where overconfident errors can be costly and difficult to detect.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a system which treats high-confidence LLM outputs as precise queries for targeted retrieval of disconfirming evidence will reduce factual and logical hallucinations more effectively than standard low-confidence-triggered RAG. This approach is inspired by the predictive coding principle where strong predictions increase precision weighting on prediction errors.
proposals.method: We will design a two-stage system: 1) A primary LLM generates an answer with a confidence score (e.g., via token probability or a separately trained verifier). 2) If confidence exceeds a tuned threshold, a verification module is activated. This module formulates the high-confidence claim into a targeted search query for a retriever (e.g., a dense passage retriever over Wikipedia or code documentation) specifically designed to find contradictory or edge-case information. The retrieved evidence is then used by an editor model (inspired by Neubig's prior work on hallucination editing) to critically assess and potentially revise the initial claim.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, neuroscience-inspired framework for confidence-based verification that inverts standard RAG logic. We will provide a new benchmark for evaluating high-confidence error correction and demonstrate its efficacy on tasks like code generation (building on TroVE) and fine-grained fact verification, advancing methods for building self-critical AI systems.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is inverting the standard confidence-retrieval link, using high confidence as a trigger for verification, which is counter-intuitive from an engineering perspective but motivated by predictive processing theory. This differs from existing calibration or post-hoc fact-checking by integrating targeted disconfirmation as a core, confidence-driven component of the generation process.

proposals.title: Stylistic Pattern Retrieval and Induction for Controllable Text Generation
proposals.motivation: Current retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems primarily retrieve factual knowledge, leaving stylistic and narrative control to prompting or fine-tuning, which are often imprecise and data-hungry. This limits applications in creative writing, personalized content, and controlled generation where the 'how' is as important as the 'what'. A method to explicitly retrieve and apply abstract stylistic patterns would enable more precise and interpretable control over text generation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by indexing text based on abstract stylistic and narrative features, we can retrieve exemplars that allow a language model to induce a transferable stylistic 'template', enabling more faithful and data-efficient stylistic transfer than prompting or fine-tuning alone.
proposals.method: We will first construct a retrieval corpus annotated with fine-grained stylistic tags (e.g., tone, narrative order, sentence complexity) using a combination of human annotation and LLM-based classifiers. We will then train a contrastive dense retriever (e.g., using a bi-encoder like Sentence-BERT) to map queries for a stylistic effect to relevant exemplars. For a given target style, retrieved exemplars will be analyzed by an LLM (e.g., GPT-4 or Llama 2) using chain-of-thought prompting to extract an explicit, abstract pattern description. This pattern will be used as a control code or in-context example to guide a generator (the same or a separate LLM) in rewriting source text.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel retrieval framework for non-factual text attributes and a methodology for inducing explicit stylistic patterns from exemplars. It will advance controllable generation by providing a more interpretable and precise mechanism for stylistic transfer, moving beyond latent control.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the conceptual shift from retrieving for factual grounding to retrieving for abstract stylistic patterns, inspired by biomimetic design in computational biology. This contrasts with existing style transfer work that relies on parallel corpora or latent space manipulations without explicit, retrievable pattern induction.

proposals.title: Learning to Retrieve or Generate: A Reinforcement Learning Framework for Adaptive Query Formulation
proposals.motivation: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) is a cornerstone of modern NLP, but systems typically use a fixed strategy for deciding when and how to query a knowledge source. This rigidity leads to inefficiency and suboptimal performance, as the utility of retrieval varies with context. A system that can intelligently choose between generating an answer directly or formulating a specific type of retrieval query is a critical step toward more adaptive and efficient language agents.
proposals.hypothesis: A reinforcement learning agent, trained with a reward based on downstream task accuracy and efficiency, can learn an effective policy to dynamically select between direct generation and a set of diverse query formulation methods, outperforming static retrieval strategies.
proposals.method: We will frame the problem as a Markov Decision Process where the state is the dialogue/document context encoded by an LLM, and actions include 'generate' or a choice from a toolbox of query methods (e.g., query prediction, question generation, claim extraction as in TroVE). The policy will be a parameterized head over the LLM's representation. We will use proximal policy optimization (PPO) with a composite reward: a primary reward for final task success (evaluated via LLM-as-judge debate for robustness) and a penalty for unnecessary retrievals. The agent will be trained and evaluated across diverse QA and code generation tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a general, trainable framework for adaptive retrieval that jointly optimizes the 'if' and 'how' of querying. It will advance the understanding of how to meta-evaluate and integrate multiple query formulation techniques within a single agent. The resulting policy and analysis will provide concrete design principles for building more efficient, context-aware RAG systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored learning to retrieve, this proposal uniquely unifies the decision to retrieve with the choice of *how* to formulate the query from a learned toolbox of methods, moving beyond single, fixed query paradigms. The integration of fine-grained efficiency penalties and scalable meta-evaluation for reward design is also novel.

proposals.title: Beyond Next-Sentence Prediction: A Meta-Evaluation of Query Formulation Methods for Forward-Looking Information Retrieval
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to generate search queries for retrieving future-oriented information, but current methods often rely on simplistic next-sentence prediction. This fails to capture the diverse intents and structures inherent in forward-looking questions, limiting the quality and relevance of retrieved information. A systematic investigation into alternative query formulation techniques is needed to improve the robustness and effectiveness of LLM-driven information seeking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that query formulation methods based on structured decomposition (e.g., question-answer pairs, programmatic tool generation) and multi-perspective debate will produce more verifiable and effective forward-looking queries than standard continuation-based generation, as measured by retrieval performance and human evaluation of relevance.
proposals.method: We will design a benchmark of forward-looking information needs across domains (e.g., technology, policy). For each, we will generate queries using: 1) baseline next-sentence prediction, 2) question decomposition inspired by QA frameworks, 3) programmatic query synthesis via induced toolboxes (extending TroVE), and 4) multi-agent debate to refine query intent. We will evaluate using retrieval metrics (nDCG, precision) on a curated corpus and conduct fine-grained human evaluation of hallucination and relevance, applying meta-evaluation techniques from prior work on LLMs as evaluators.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic taxonomy and evaluation framework for forward-looking query generation. It will deliver actionable insights into which formulation methods work best for different types of information needs, advancing the state of LLM-augmented retrieval. The resulting benchmark and analysis will inform the design of more reliable, intent-aware search systems.
proposals.novelty: While query reformulation is well-studied for factual retrieval, applying structured program synthesis and evaluator debate specifically to the under-explored problem of forward-looking query generation is novel. The integration of verifiable tool use and meta-evaluation for this task moves beyond simple prompt engineering.

proposals.title: Ensemble-Based Uncertainty for Adaptive Retrieval in Language Models
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) are prone to generating confident but incorrect or hallucinated content, especially for knowledge-intensive tasks. Current retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems often rely on a single model's token-level confidence or simple heuristics to trigger retrieval, which fails to capture deeper epistemic uncertainty about the correct generation path. A more robust, calibrated measure of uncertainty is needed to make retrieval activation more reliable and efficient.
proposals.hypothesis: An ensemble of diverse LLMs, by providing a distribution over possible outputs, yields a more robust uncertainty signal than any single model, and using this signal to trigger retrieval will improve the accuracy and reliability of generation on knowledge-intensive tasks.
proposals.method: We will construct an ensemble of pre-trained language models varying in architecture (e.g., decoder-only vs. encoder-decoder), scale, and training data (e.g., GPT, Llama, T5). For a given prompt, we will compute the variance or entropy across the output probability distributions of each ensemble member. This aggregated uncertainty metric will be thresholded to decide when to query a retrieval system (e.g., a dense vector index). We will evaluate this approach on established QA and fact-checking benchmarks, comparing against single-model confidence baselines and fixed-interval retrieval.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, ensemble-driven method for quantifying generation uncertainty in LLMs, directly applicable to improving retrieval-augmented systems. It will yield empirical evidence on how model diversity affects uncertainty calibration and offer a practical, scalable framework for making RAG systems more trustworthy and efficient.
proposals.novelty: While ensembles are known for improving prediction accuracy, their systematic use for quantifying uncertainty to *gate* external actions like retrieval in LLMs is novel. This adapts the core principle of Bayesian Model Averaging—trusting the spread of an ensemble—from fields like climate forecasting to the problem of reliable knowledge access in language generation.

proposals.title: Amortized Prediction of Information Gain for Low-Latency Retrieval-Augmented Generation
proposals.motivation: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) improves LLM factuality but introduces significant latency from retrieval and context processing. Current heuristics for triggering retrieval, like fixed intervals or simple entropy thresholds, are not grounded in the expected utility of retrieved information, leading to inefficient or ineffective use of external knowledge. A principled, information-theoretic trigger is needed but is computationally prohibitive for real-time use.
proposals.hypothesis: A small, amortized neural network can accurately predict the information gain of a potential retrieval step using only efficient contextual features from the current generation state, enabling near-optimal retrieval decisions with negligible added latency.
proposals.method: We will first construct a dataset of generation episodes (text/code) where, for each step, we compute the true information gain (e.g., reduction in predictive entropy or KL divergence) from oracle retrievals. We then train a lightweight predictor (e.g., a 2-layer MLP or TinyBERT) on this data, using features like the current decoder hidden state, recent token entropy, and topic/cluster embeddings derived from recent context. This model will be integrated into a RAG system to make binary retrieval decisions, with its predictions calibrated against a latency budget.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical, theoretically-grounded triggering mechanism for RAG that minimizes latency overhead. It will also provide a novel framework for evaluating retrieval utility in dynamic generation contexts, advancing the meta-evaluation of tool-using LLMs.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses retrieval heuristics or post-hoc evaluation, this method proactively amortizes a complex information-theoretic calculation into a fast neural inference, making real-time, gain-aware triggering feasible for the first time.

proposals.title: Progressive Overload for Robust Neural Retrieval: A Curriculum Learning Framework
proposals.motivation: Neural retrievers, such as dense passage retrievers (DPR) and late-interaction models (e.g., ColBERT), are central to open-domain QA and RAG systems, but their performance often plateaus or degrades on complex, multi-hop queries. Standard training on static, uniformly difficult datasets fails to systematically strengthen the model's core retrieval capability, analogous to training an athlete without a structured regimen. This work addresses the need for more adaptive and robust training paradigms for retrieval models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a curriculum learning strategy inspired by the sports science principle of progressive overload—systematically increasing retrieval task difficulty along dimensions like corpus size, distractor density, and reasoning complexity—will yield neural retrievers with superior robustness and generalization compared to models trained on fixed-difficulty datasets.
proposals.method: We will develop a curriculum generator that creates training episodes of escalating difficulty for a dual-encoder retriever. Starting with a small, clean corpus and unambiguous queries, we will progressively increase: 1) corpus size and semantic distractors, 2) noise in query formulation (e.g., paraphrases, typos), and 3) the need for multi-hop reasoning by decomposing complex questions. The model's performance on a validation set of 'challenge' episodes will dictate the pacing of difficulty increases. We will evaluate the final model on benchmarks like MultiHopQA and BEIR, using both automatic metrics and human evaluation of retrieved passage relevance.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, principled training framework for neural retrieval that moves beyond static datasets. It will provide empirical evidence for the benefits of structured difficulty escalation in ML training and deliver retrieval models with demonstrably improved robustness to distribution shift and complex query types.
proposals.novelty: While curriculum learning is known, its application to retrieval has been limited to simple heuristics like passage length. This proposal novelly formalizes the 'progressive overload' analogy, defining a multi-dimensional difficulty space (corpus, signal, reasoning) specifically for retrieval, and dynamically adjusts the curriculum based on model performance.

proposals.title: FLARE-Lite: Low-Latency, Verifiable Text Generation via Proactive Retrieval for Short-Form Tasks
proposals.motivation: The Forward-Looking Active REtrieval (FLARE) method improves factuality by proactively retrieving information during generation, but its iterative retrieval steps introduce latency unsuitable for real-time applications like search engines or voice assistants. As LLMs are increasingly deployed in interactive settings, there is a critical need for verifiable generation that does not compromise on speed. This work addresses the trade-off between factual grounding and response time for short-form text generation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by adapting FLARE's proactive retrieval mechanism with a single-step, task-specific retrieval strategy and a lightweight verification module, we can achieve significant latency reduction for short-form generation while maintaining or improving factual accuracy compared to standard retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) baselines.
proposals.method: We will adapt the FLARE framework by: 1) Replacing its iterative retrieval with a single, parallel retrieval step triggered by a distilled 'uncertainty' classifier that predicts which initial tokens necessitate grounding, inspired by fine-grained hallucination detection work. 2) Employing a dense retriever (e.g., Contriever) over a curated knowledge source and a small, fast decoder (e.g., a distilled CodeLLaMA variant) for generation. 3) Integrating a TroVE-inspired post-generation verification step that uses lightweight, learned programmatic checks to flag potential inconsistencies in the final output without full re-generation.
proposals.contribution: This project will contribute a novel, latency-optimized architecture for verifiable short-text generation, enabling real-time use. We will provide a rigorous meta-evaluation framework, using agent debate and human assessment, to benchmark the trustworthiness-speed trade-off across QA and snippet generation tasks. The resulting model and analysis will offer practical guidelines for deploying factually grounded LLMs in low-latency environments.
proposals.novelty: While FLARE focuses on improving accuracy for long-form generation, our work uniquely re-engineers it for the low-latency constraint of short-form tasks, combining proactive retrieval with a single-step execution and lightweight verification—a combination not explored in prior retrieval-augmented generation literature.

proposals.title: Adversarial Curriculum Learning for Retrieval Corpus Expansion via Targeted Synthetic Data Generation
proposals.motivation: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) is critical for grounding LLMs, but its performance is bottlenecked by corpus coverage. Existing corpora are static and may lack documents for future, unseen queries, especially in dynamic or low-resource domains. This leads to retrieval failures and subsequent model hallucination, a core problem in Neubig's recent work on fine-grained hallucination detection.
proposals.hypothesis: An adversarial curriculum learning loop, where a generator synthesizes documents targeting a retriever's current failure modes, can systematically expand a corpus's effective coverage and improve retrieval robustness for active generation tasks more efficiently than scaling with raw, untargeted data.
proposals.method: We adapt the epitope generation analogy into a concrete framework. We initialize a dense retriever (e.g., DPR or Contriever) and a generator (a fine-tuned T5 or GPT-2). In each curriculum round, we use the retriever's low-confidence retrievals or embedding space clustering to identify failure modes. The generator, conditioned on these 'adversarial' queries or latent prototypes, produces synthetic documents. These are added to the corpus, and the retriever is fine-tuned on a mix of real and synthetic data. The process iterates, with the generator's objective adjusted via reinforcement learning or gradient-based adversarial training to create progressively harder, valid synthetic documents.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel adversarial curriculum learning method for retrieval corpus expansion. 2) A systematic, task-aware alternative to massive, undirected corpus scaling. 3) New evaluation protocols, inspired by meta-evaluation via agent debate, to measure coverage and robustness gains against synthetic adversarial queries.
proposals.novelty: While data augmentation and adversarial training exist, applying a *curricular*, *closed-loop* adversarial process between a generator and retriever for systematic corpus expansion is novel. It directly translates a biological vaccine design strategy to NLP, moving beyond static corpora towards self-improving, task-adaptive retrieval systems.

proposals.title: AGI: Adversarially Grounded Indexing via Multi-Agent Scrutiny for Robust Retrieval-Augmented Generation
proposals.motivation: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems are prone to amplifying the biases and hallucinations of the single language model used to create their retrieval index. As of 2025, there is a critical need for methods that make the indexing process itself more verifiable and robust, preventing the propagation of errors into the system's foundational knowledge structure. This is especially crucial for code and factual knowledge tasks, where errors in retrieval can cascade.
proposals.hypothesis: An adversarial multi-agent framework, where a generator's indexing proposals are scrutinized by an independent critic model, will produce retrieval structures that are significantly more robust to hallucinations and biases than those built by a single model.
proposals.method: We will instantiate a generator LLM (e.g., CodeLlama) to propose document clusters, keyphrases, or metadata tags for a corpus. A separate critic model (e.g., a fine-tuned model using techniques from TroVE and hallucination detection papers) will evaluate each proposal. The critic will use structured debate and verification against a trusted knowledge sub-corpus, checking for contradictions, hallucinations, and coverage gaps. Only proposals passing a verifiability threshold will be committed to a dynamic, versioned index. The process will be iterated, treating the generator's output as hypotheses for adversarial stress-testing.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel framework for building auditable and self-correcting retrieval indices. 2) New benchmarks and meta-evaluation protocols, extending agent-debate methods, for assessing the factual grounding of indexing strategies. 3) Empirical evidence on how adversarial scrutiny improves downstream task performance in code generation and open-domain QA.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial training and multi-agent debate exist for text generation, applying this paradigm to *control the construction of a retrieval index* is novel. It shifts the adversarial objective from improving a single output to improving the system's persistent knowledge infrastructure.

proposals.title: TABLENQ: Generating Natural Language Queries from Tables for LLM Evaluation and Augmentation
proposals.motivation: Current table QA research focuses on mapping natural language (NL) to SQL or direct answers, creating a one-way evaluation paradigm. This limits our ability to systematically assess and improve model understanding of complex table structures and semantics. Generating diverse, realistic NL queries from tables inverts this process, enabling the creation of large-scale, high-quality diagnostic benchmarks and data augmentation for table-based reasoning.
proposals.hypothesis: Large language models (LLMs), guided by structured constraints and fine-grained feedback, can generate fluent, valid, and semantically diverse natural language questions from relational tables, and these generated queries will provide a robust, scalable resource for meta-evaluation and training data augmentation.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-stage pipeline: 1) Use a pre-trained LLM (e.g., CodeLlama) as a base, conditioned on table schema and sampled rows. 2) Introduce a constrained decoding or fine-tuning stage using formal representations (e.g., SQL or SPARQL sketches) to ensure logical validity, inspired by TroVE's programmatic induction. 3) Implement an iterative refinement loop where a separate 'critic' LLM, trained via debate-style meta-evaluation, scores and edits queries for fluency, relevance, and answerability, applying hallucination detection techniques. 4) Cluster generated queries by linguistic and logical patterns to analyze diversity.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel framework and dataset for inverting the table QA task, providing a new tool for the meta-evaluation of table-understanding LLMs. It will also demonstrate how generated queries can augment training data for improved robustness in downstream tasks like semantic parsing and open-domain QA over tables.
proposals.novelty: While query generation exists in limited database contexts, our approach uniquely integrates LLM-based generation with programmatic constraints and meta-evaluation via agent debate to ensure quality and diversity, directly inverting the standard evaluation paradigm for a critical data modality.

proposals.title: TaSeL: Table-to-Text and Schema Learning via Self-Supervised Objectives for Data-Efficient Semantic Parsing
proposals.motivation: Training models for tasks like text-to-SQL or table QA currently requires large, expensive datasets of parallel natural language and table data. This bottleneck limits the application of semantic parsers to new domains and low-resource languages. A method that can leverage the vast, unpaired corpora of standalone text documents and standalone tables available on the web would dramatically improve data efficiency and model generalization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by pre-training a single encoder-decoder model with novel, separate self-supervised objectives on unpaired text and unpaired tables, we can learn rich, aligned representations that enable strong few-shot and zero-shot performance on downstream semantic parsing tasks without paired supervision.
proposals.method: We will adapt a T5 or BART architecture. For the table-only stream, we will design objectives like corrupted schema recovery (masking column headers/types/relations) and synthetic NL description generation from table content using templates. For the text-only stream, we will use a novel 'synthetic table generation' objective, where the model must produce a plausible table schema and key-value pairs implied by the text. A contrastive loss will align representations from the two streams in a shared latent space, using heuristics like entity overlap for weak alignment signals.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a new pre-training framework for data-efficient semantic parsing, 2) a public model pre-trained on large-scale unpaired WebTables and Wikipedia text, and 3) a rigorous meta-evaluation protocol, inspired by Neubig's prior work on LLM evaluation, to measure the trustworthiness of model outputs on held-out tasks like Spider and WikiTableQuestions.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work on table pre-training (e.g., TAPAS, TaBERT) which relies on paired data or simple heuristics to create it, our method completely decouples the text and table pre-training phases, using only self-supervision within each modality before learning to align them, a more scalable and flexible paradigm.

proposals.title: Scaling and Diversifying Table-Centric Pretraining: A Study on Corpus Size and Multilinguality for Data-to-Text and Semantic Parsing
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, LLMs show promise in understanding and generating structured data like tables, but their performance is often limited by the scale and linguistic diversity of their pretraining corpora, which are predominantly English web text. This restricts their utility for global applications and complex data-to-text tasks. Scaling table pretraining with larger, multilingual corpora could unlock more robust reasoning and generation capabilities across languages.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pretraining language models on a significantly larger and multilingual corpus of tables will lead to measurable improvements in (1) the factual fidelity and coherence of generated text from tables, and (2) the cross-lingual transfer performance for semantic parsing tasks, compared to models trained on existing English-only table datasets.
proposals.method: We will construct a massive multilingual table-text corpus by extracting and aligning tables from high-quality, multilingual web sources (e.g., Wikipedia) and existing datasets like WikiTableQuestions and ToTTo. We will then continue pretraining a base LLM (e.g., CodeLlama, given its structural awareness) using a masked table infilling objective and a parallel table-to-text generation objective. The resulting models will be rigorously evaluated using Neubig's recent meta-evaluation frameworks (e.g., agent debate for text quality) and fine-grained hallucination detection methods, across established benchmarks in English and multiple other languages.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first large-scale empirical analysis of the effects of table corpus scale and multilinguality on LLM capabilities. It will produce and release a novel multilingual table pretraining corpus and a set of models with enhanced, verifiable table understanding, directly contributing to more reliable data-to-text systems and tools for programmatic task solving (e.g., TroVE) in a global context.
proposals.novelty: While scaling text corpora is common, systematically scaling and diversifying *table* corpora for pretraining is unexplored. The novelty lies in the joint focus on scale *and* multilinguality for structured data, coupled with a rigorous, pre-2026 evaluation methodology focused on trustworthiness and hallucination to assess the true gains.

proposals.title: Inducing Verifiable Parsers: Program Synthesis for Robust and Interpretable Text-to-SQL Generation
proposals.motivation: Current neural text-to-SQL models, while powerful, can be brittle and opaque, generating incorrect queries that are difficult to debug. This is a critical barrier for reliable database interaction and trustworthy language model applications. A synthesis-based approach that learns explicit, verifiable parsing rules could improve robustness, data efficiency, and provide clear failure modes.
proposals.hypothesis: By framing text-to-SQL as a program induction task, we can learn a compact set of interpretable, logic-based parsing rules that generalize more robustly from limited data than end-to-end neural models, especially for compositional queries and domain-specific schemas.
proposals.method: We will adapt and extend neural program induction frameworks, like those in TroVE, to the parsing domain. The method involves: 1) Using a large language model (LLM) as a weak supervisor to generate candidate SQL parses and logical forms for natural language utterances, creating a silver-standard dataset. 2) Applying inductive logic programming (ILP) techniques, such as meta-interpretive learning (MIL), to induce a minimal, generalizable logic program (parser) from this data. 3) Implementing a meta-evaluation debate protocol, inspired by agent debate work, where multiple LLM agents critique induced rules for consistency and coverage against held-out examples.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel hybrid methodology for semantic parsing that combines the generative power of LLMs with the rigor of symbolic program synthesis. It will produce a new benchmark for evaluating the verifiability and robustness of text-to-SQL systems, moving beyond execution accuracy to include rule interpretability and compositional generalization.
proposals.novelty: While ILP and neural program synthesis exist, their application to *inducing parsers directly from text* using LLMs as a weak labeling source is novel. The integration of scalable meta-evaluation via agent debate to critique the synthesized logic programs is a new direction for ensuring the quality of induced rules.

proposals.title: T-CLIP: Aligning Tabular and Textual Representations via Contrastive Learning for Faithful Data-to-Text Generation
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) frequently hallucinate when generating textual summaries from structured data like tables, producing unsupported or incorrect facts. Existing fine-tuning and prompting methods struggle to enforce strict faithfulness, as they lack a robust, learned semantic alignment between the discrete, relational structure of tables and fluent text. This gap hinders reliable deployment in high-stakes domains like finance and scientific reporting.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting contrastive learning objectives, proven for vision-language alignment (e.g., in CLIP), to explicitly align table and text representations within a shared embedding space will significantly improve the faithfulness and controllability of data-to-text generation by LLMs.
proposals.method: We will construct a large-scale dataset of (table, faithful description) pairs from sources like Wikipedia infoboxes and scientific papers. A dual-encoder architecture will be trained: a table encoder (based on TAPAS or a graph neural network) and a text encoder (from a pre-trained LLM). We will employ a contrastive loss (InfoNCE) to pull matching table-text pairs close in embedding space while pushing non-matching pairs apart. The aligned encoders will then be integrated into an LLM for generation via retrieval-augmented prompting or as a faithfulness reward model for fine-tuning, with evaluation using metrics from our prior work on fine-grained hallucination detection.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) T-CLIP, a novel model for table-text alignment, 2) a rigorous meta-evaluation framework (extending agent-debate methods) to assess the faithfulness of generated text against source tables, and 3) new state-of-the-art results on data-to-text faithfulness benchmarks, advancing towards verifiable text generation.
proposals.novelty: While contrastive alignment is established for images and text, its systematic application to the structured, heterogeneous modality of tables is novel. This approach moves beyond treating tables as linearized text, explicitly modeling their structure to prevent hallucination, a core challenge identified in our prior editing and toolbox induction research.

proposals.title: Retrieval-Augmented Table Parsing: Improving Accuracy and Scope with Dynamic Snippet Retrieval
proposals.motivation: Parsing complex tables into structured formats (e.g., code, logical forms) is a critical task for data integration and question answering, but current methods often fail on tables with unseen schemas or implicit domain knowledge. Pre-2026, large language models (LLMs) can struggle with such tasks due to parametric knowledge limits and hallucination. Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) has shown promise for text, but its application to structured table parsing remains underexplored and could address these accuracy and scope limitations.
proposals.hypothesis: Dynamically retrieving relevant table snippets (e.g., similar headers, cell values, or schema descriptions) from a corpus during parsing will provide contextual cues that significantly improve the accuracy and schema coverage of LLM-based table parsers, especially for out-of-domain or complex tables.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage RAG system for table parsing. First, a dense retriever (based on pre-2026 models like Contriever or BGE) will index a corpus of diverse tables and their associated metadata. Given a query table, it will retrieve the top-k relevant snippets (table slices, column descriptions). Second, an LLM parser (e.g., CodeLlama or GPT-4) will be prompted with both the query table and these retrieved snippets to generate the target output (e.g., Python pandas code, SQL, or a logical form). We will evaluate using existing benchmarks (e.g., WikiTableQuestions, TabFact) and a novel meta-evaluation protocol inspired by Neubig's prior work on agent debate to assess the verifiability of improvements.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, generalizable framework for retrieval-augmented table parsing that demonstrably improves accuracy on complex and out-of-domain tables. It will also contribute a rigorous meta-evaluation of how retrieval mitigates specific types of hallucination in structured data tasks, extending the fine-grained editing and evaluation themes from the researcher's prior work.
proposals.novelty: While RAG is known for text, its tailored application to table parsing—with retrieval of structured snippets and integration into a parsing pipeline—is novel. The combination with programmatic output generation and verifiable evaluation directly connects to the researcher's expertise in code, languages, and scalable meta-evaluation.

proposals.title: META-TABLE: Meta-Learning for Few-Shot Adaptation to Novel Table Domains
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) struggle with structured reasoning over tables from unseen domains, requiring extensive fine-tuning data. In pre-2026 NLP, few-shot learning for semantic parsing, question answering, and data imputation on tables remains a significant challenge, as models fail to generalize across diverse schemas and linguistic patterns with minimal examples. This limits the deployment of automated systems in data-rich but label-poor environments like scientific databases or enterprise spreadsheets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a meta-learning framework, which explicitly trains a model to rapidly adapt its table-understanding capabilities, will significantly outperform standard fine-tuning and in-context learning when adapting to novel table domains with only a few labeled examples.
proposals.method: We will develop META-TABLE, a model-agnostic meta-learner (MAML) applied to a T5 or CodeT5 backbone. The method involves constructing a meta-dataset of tables from diverse public sources (e.g., WikiTableQuestions, TabFact). During meta-training, tasks will be defined as adaptation to a specific table domain for a target operation (e.g., column type annotation, SQL generation). The inner loop will perform few-shot gradient updates on a support set, and the outer loop will update the model's initial parameters to optimize performance on a query set across all meta-training tasks. We will rigorously evaluate adaptation speed and final accuracy on held-out table domains.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a robust, sample-efficient method for adapting LLMs to new structured data contexts, reducing the need for costly human annotation. It will also provide new insights into the meta-learnability of schema- and language-agnostic table representations, advancing few-shot learning for structured data.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning has been explored for NLP tasks, its application to few-shot domain adaptation for table-specific reasoning is novel. This approach moves beyond simple prompt engineering or adapter modules, directly optimizing the model's initialization for rapid convergence on unseen table structures and semantics.

proposals.title: TABGEN: Evaluating and Improving Table Generation from Text via LLM-Based Meta-Evaluation and Constrained Decoding
proposals.motivation: Automatically generating structured tables from descriptive text is crucial for knowledge extraction, data wrangling, and improving human-AI collaboration in data analysis. However, pre-2026 research primarily focuses on generating text from tables, with the inverse task being under-explored and lacking robust, scalable evaluation methods. This creates a significant gap in creating reliable, verifiable structured outputs from unstructured language.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a large language model (LLM), fine-tuned with a constrained decoding mechanism and guided by a scalable, multi-agent meta-evaluation framework, can generate accurate, well-structured, and faithful tables from descriptive paragraphs, outperforming naive prompting and standard fine-tuning approaches.
proposals.method: We will first curate a dataset of (paragraph, table) pairs from existing sources like Wikipedia infoboxes and scientific papers. We will fine-tune a decoder-only LLM (e.g., CodeLlama) on this task, treating table generation as a structured sequence-to-sequence problem. Inspired by TroVE, we will implement a constrained decoding module to enforce valid table syntax and structure during generation. For evaluation, we will adapt the agent debate framework from Neubig's prior work, using multiple LLM 'agents' to critique generated tables for faithfulness to the source text and structural correctness, creating a scalable meta-evaluation metric.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a novel model and method for high-fidelity text-to-table generation, 2) a new, scalable evaluation protocol for structured generation tasks using multi-agent meta-evaluation, and 3) a public benchmark dataset and model to advance research in structured knowledge extraction and hallucination reduction.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic integration of constrained decoding for programmatic output (TROVE) with a rigorous, debate-based meta-evaluation framework (from evaluator trust work) specifically for the under-addressed inverse task of table generation, moving beyond simple text-to-SQL or template filling.

proposals.title: BARTScore-Zero: Reference-Free Evaluation of Generated Text via Consistency with a Latent World Model
proposals.motivation: Metrics like BARTScore require parallel source-reference text, limiting their use in many real-world generation tasks where the source is unavailable (e.g., creative writing, open-ended dialogue, or code generation from informal specs). Pre-2026, reference-free evaluation often relies on simple heuristics or requires training on task-specific human judgments, which is not scalable. A method to assess text quality based solely on internal consistency and alignment with a general world model would greatly expand the scope of automatic evaluation.
proposals.hypothesis: The quality and factuality of a generated text can be effectively estimated by measuring its internal consistency and its consistency with a latent 'world model' induced from a large language model, without access to the source input or gold references.
proposals.method: We adapt the BARTScore framework by replacing the source-conditioned log-probability with a novel scoring function. First, we decompose the generated text into atomic claims via an LLM-based semantic parser. We then compute two scores using a frozen, pre-trained LLM (e.g., T5 or BART): (1) an *internal consistency* score, measuring the probability of each claim given other claims in the text, and (2) a *world model consistency* score, measuring the probability of each claim given a large retrieved or generated knowledge context from the LLM. The final score is a calibrated weighted sum, optimized on a small set of human judgments for robustness across tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable, reference- and source-free metric for text generation evaluation, directly applicable to open-ended tasks. It will contribute a novel framework for using LLMs as implicit world models for verification, advancing the field of meta-evaluation. The resulting metric, BARTScore-Zero, will be validated across diverse tasks (summarization, dialogue, data-to-text) using human studies and correlation analyses.
proposals.novelty: Unlike BARTScore, our method requires no parallel source text. Unlike prior reference-free work, it systematically uses the LLM's internal knowledge as a verifiable world model rather than relying on surface-level features or task-specific fine-tuning.

proposals.title: META-MM: Meta-Evaluating Multimodal LLM Evaluators via Agent Debate and Cross-Modal Consistency
proposals.motivation: The evaluation of text generated in response to multimodal inputs (e.g., images, tables) remains a critical, unsolved challenge. Pre-2026, LLMs are increasingly used as scalable, reference-free evaluators for text, but their application to multimodal contexts naively assumes text-only evaluation, ignoring the grounding context. This limits the reliability of automated evaluation for tasks like image captioning, visual question answering, and data-to-text generation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating the source multimodal context directly into the evaluation-generation process of an LLM, and meta-evaluating these judgments via structured agent debate, will yield more verifiable and accurate assessments of multimodal text generation than text-only evaluation protocols.
proposals.method: We will extend the agent debate framework from Neubig's prior work to multimodal settings. A generator LLM will produce an evaluation critique, conditioned on both the candidate text and the source modality (e.g., via a vision encoder for images or a structured linearizer for tables). A verifier LLM will then assess this critique, also with multimodal access. We will measure cross-modal consistency—disagreement when the source modality is omitted—as a key meta-evaluation signal. The approach will be tested on curated benchmarks from VALSE (hallucination) and established multimodal generation tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel, scalable framework for meta-evaluating multimodal LLM-as-a-judge systems, 2) new insights into the necessity of cross-modal grounding for faithful evaluation, and 3) a publicly released benchmark suite for multimodal evaluation meta-assessment, advancing towards more trustworthy automated evaluation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work meta-evaluates text-only LLM evaluators and detects hallucinations in unimodal text, this proposal uniquely integrates multimodal grounding into the evaluator generation loop and uses agent debate to establish verifiable, cross-modally consistent judgments.

proposals.title: Specialized Metric Tuning: Fine-tuning BARTScore on Human Judgments for Granular Evaluation of Fluency and Factuality
proposals.motivation: Current reference-based evaluation metrics like BARTScore provide a general-purpose score but often correlate poorly with specific human judgment dimensions like fluency or factuality. As LLMs are increasingly used for high-stakes generation tasks, there is a critical need for automated metrics that reliably and specifically assess these distinct quality aspects, reducing reliance on costly and slow human evaluation.
