proposals.hypothesis: Fine-tuning BARTScore on targeted, dimension-specific human judgment datasets will yield specialized metrics that achieve significantly higher correlation with human ratings for their target dimension (e.g., fluency or factuality) compared to the general-purpose BARTScore and other baseline metrics.
proposals.method: We will collect or curate existing human-annotated datasets where judgments are explicitly split by dimension (e.g., SummEval for coherence/fluency, FRANK for factuality). We will fine-tune separate BART-large models on each dimension's data, using the human score as the regression target for the candidate text, conditioned on the source/reference. We will rigorously evaluate using meta-evaluation techniques from recent work (e.g., agent debate for scalable assessment), measuring the Pearson/Spearman correlation of each fine-tuned variant against held-out human judgments, both in-domain and across diverse generation tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a suite of publicly available, specialized evaluation metrics for key generation quality dimensions. It will provide a clear methodology for adapting general-purpose metric backbones to specific evaluation needs and offer new insights into the transferability and task-specificity of learned metric representations.
proposals.novelty: While BARTScore has been used as-is and recent work explores LLMs as evaluators, the systematic fine-tuning of a reference-based metric backbone on distinct human judgment dimensions to create a family of specialized metrics is a novel and targeted approach to improving automated evaluation granularity.

proposals.title: Contrastive Latent Alignment for Hallucination Detection and Faithful Text Generation
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) frequently generate plausible but unfaithful text, a core challenge for safe and reliable deployment. Existing hallucination detection and editing methods often rely on brittle, token-level comparisons or external knowledge, lacking a robust, intrinsic measure of semantic alignment between generated and reference content. A method to learn a latent space where faithful and hallucinated text are systematically separable would significantly improve model trustworthiness.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying contrastive learning objectives, inspired by successes in vision (e.g., CLIP, SimCLR), to align the latent representations of generated text with their corresponding reference texts—while pushing apart representations of hallucinated variants—will create a more semantically structured embedding space that enables more accurate and fine-grained hallucination detection and editing.
proposals.method: We will adapt a contrastive learning framework (e.g., InfoNCE loss) to a dual-encoder architecture. One encoder processes reference text (or code), while another processes model-generated output. Positive pairs will be (reference, faithful generation); negative pairs will be constructed by pairing the reference with (a) generations from other samples and (b) intentionally hallucinated versions of the current generation (created via methods from prior hallucination editing work). We will integrate this objective into the training of models like T5 or CodeT5, evaluating on tasks from the TroVE and hallucination detection paper benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel training paradigm for improving the intrinsic faithfulness of text and code generation. It will yield a model with a latent space directly optimized for verifiability, enabling more efficient and accurate hallucination detection without heavy reliance on external tools or human judges, advancing towards self-evaluating models.
proposals.novelty: While contrastive learning is established in vision and multimodal settings, its systematic application to align mono-modal text representations for hallucination mitigation is novel. The key innovation is the structured construction of negative samples using hallucinated text, moving beyond simple in-batch negatives to teach the model the specific concept of 'unfaithfulness'.

proposals.title: META-SCOPE: A Large-Scale, Multi-Domain Benchmark for the Meta-Evaluation of Unsupervised Text Generation Metrics
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of reference-free metrics like BARTScore, BLEURT, and others for evaluating generated text (summarization, translation, dialogue) has outpaced rigorous assessment of their reliability. Current meta-evaluation relies on small, domain-specific human judgment datasets (e.g., SummEval, WMT Metrics), limiting our understanding of metric biases, robustness, and generalizability across diverse tasks and languages. A comprehensive, large-scale benchmark is needed to systematically stress-test these metrics and guide future development.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a large-scale, multi-domain meta-evaluation benchmark will reveal significant, previously undetected failures of unsupervised metrics, particularly in their consistency across languages, sensitivity to fine-grained error types (e.g., hallucination vs. redundancy), and correlation with human judgment on creative or complex generation tasks.
proposals.method: We will construct META-SCOPE by aggregating and extending existing human-annotated datasets (e.g., from GEM, TRUE, DynaBench) across text summarization, dialogue, data-to-text, and code generation. We will apply clustering techniques (inspired by Neubig's work on language and code clustering) to ensure diversity in error types and linguistic phenomena. The core method involves a systematic meta-evaluation protocol: for each dataset instance (source, human reference, system outputs, human scores), we will compute scores from a suite of target metrics (BARTScore, BLEURT, etc.) and measure their correlation (e.g., Kendall's Tau) with human judgments, analyzing variance across domains and error clusters.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the NLP community with 1) a standardized, large-scale resource for meta-evaluation, 2) a detailed analysis of the strengths and failure modes of current unsupervised metrics, and 3) a framework for developing more robust, generalizable evaluation metrics, directly impacting evaluation practices for LLMs.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior meta-evaluation efforts focused on single tasks or languages, META-SCOPE's novelty lies in its scale, systematic construction via clustering for diversity, and its explicit focus on cross-domain and cross-lingual generalization of metric performance, moving beyond aggregate correlation reports.

proposals.title: Mining Complex Knowledge Structures: Automated Prompt Generation for Procedures, Constraints, and Heuristics
proposals.motivation: Current automated prompt mining techniques, such as those for generating knowledge graphs, excel at extracting simple factual triples (e.g., 'Paris is capital of France'). However, pre-2026 LLMs possess knowledge of more complex structures like multi-step procedures, conditional constraints, and problem-solving heuristics, which are poorly captured by existing methods. Extracting this structured, procedural knowledge is crucial for building more reliable, verifiable, and instructable AI systems, particularly in domains like code generation and task planning.
proposals.hypothesis: By extending prompt mining frameworks to target complex knowledge types—specifically procedural workflows, contextual constraints, and domain-specific heuristics—we can automatically generate high-quality prompts that elicit this knowledge from LLMs more reliably than manually crafted prompts or simple triple-based mining.
proposals.method: We will extend frameworks like APE (Automatic Prompt Engineer) and its successors. First, we define novel output specifications for complex types (e.g., a DSL for procedures). Next, we employ an iterative generate-and-cluster approach: using an LLM as a generator, we produce candidate prompts, execute them to obtain knowledge samples, and then use the researcher's expertise in clustering and meta-evaluation (inspired by 'Agent Debate' and 'TROVE') to cluster and score outputs. High-scoring clusters will inform prompt refinement via back-translation or gradient-based methods, with final evaluation across held-out tasks requiring procedural or constrained reasoning.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide 1) a novel methodology and benchmark for mining complex knowledge prompts, 2) a dataset of high-quality prompts for procedures, constraints, and heuristics across domains like coding and everyday tasks, and 3) an analysis of how elicitation strategies differ for complex versus simple knowledge, advancing the science of knowledge extraction from LLMs.
proposals.novelty: While prior prompt mining focuses on factual relations, this proposal targets the uncharted territory of automated extraction for procedural and conditional knowledge. The integration of clustering-based meta-evaluation and a toolbox-induction mindset (from TROVE) to curate and verify mined prompts for these complex types is a novel synthesis.

proposals.title: Probing the Depths: Adversarial Prompt Generation for Systematic Knowledge Elicitation in Large Language Models
proposals.motivation: Evaluating the true knowledge boundaries of large language models (LLMs) remains a critical, unsolved challenge. Standard prompting often fails to reveal latent knowledge or hidden biases, leading to unreliable assessments of model capabilities and safety. This work addresses the need for rigorous, automated meta-evaluation techniques to stress-test model understanding, building directly on Neubig's recent research into scalable LLM evaluation and hallucination detection.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an adversarial generation framework, which iteratively crafts prompts to exploit model inconsistencies and decision boundaries, can systematically elicit a more complete and verifiable representation of a model's knowledge across linguistic and programmatic domains than standard prompting methods.
proposals.method: We will develop an adversarial agent that uses gradient-based or discrete optimization methods (e.g., adapted from HotFlip or AutoPrompt) to generate input perturbations. This agent will engage in a multi-turn 'debate' or probing dialogue with a target LLM, guided by a reward function that maximizes the divergence between the target model's outputs and verified ground-truth knowledge bases (for facts) or executable unit tests (for code). The approach will be evaluated across diverse tasks from the TroVE programmatic benchmark and fine-grained hallucination detection datasets.
proposals.contribution: This project will yield a novel, scalable method for automated knowledge boundary detection in LLMs, contributing a new benchmark for model trustworthiness. It will also produce a public dataset of adversarial prompts that effectively reveal model failures, aiding in the development of more robust and interpretable models.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial attacks exist, applying them as a systematic *diagnostic tool* for knowledge elicitation—particularly in conjunction with programmatic verification from TroVE and meta-evaluation via agent debate—is a novel synthesis. This moves beyond finding failures to creating a structured probe of model understanding.

proposals.title: Compositional Prompt Generation for Multi-Hop Knowledge Probing via LLM Agent Debate
proposals.motivation: Current automatic prompt generation methods excel at single-step tasks but struggle with complex, multi-hop reasoning where an answer requires chaining multiple pieces of knowledge. This limits our ability to reliably evaluate and understand the true reasoning capabilities and knowledge boundaries of Large Language Models (LLMs). Addressing this gap is crucial for building trustworthy, verifiable systems for complex question answering and code generation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-agent debate framework, guided by fine-grained hallucination detection, can generate and refine compositional prompts that systematically probe an LLM's ability to perform reliable multi-step reasoning, revealing failure modes that single-step probes miss.
proposals.method: We will extend automatic prompt generation by treating prompt composition as a programmatic task. Inspired by TroVE, we will induce a verifiable 'toolbox' of prompt operators for knowledge retrieval, logical composition, and constraint checking. An agent debate meta-evaluation framework, building on Neubig's prior work, will generate candidate multi-hop prompts, while a fine-grained hallucination detector will critique intermediate reasoning steps. This iterative process will produce a benchmark of complex, validated probes.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel methodology for generating verifiable, multi-step reasoning probes, 2) a new benchmark for evaluating LLMs on compositional knowledge tasks, and 3) insights into the specific failure modes (e.g., missing links, logical inconsistencies) that emerge during multi-hop reasoning in both language and code domains.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synthesis of agent-based meta-evaluation and fine-grained hallucination editing specifically for the *generation* of complex evaluation tasks, rather than just for solving them, creating a closed-loop system for scalable, trustworthy assessment of compositional reasoning.

proposals.title: Specialized over Scaled: A Meta-Evaluation of Task-Specific vs. General-Purpose LLMs for Code Generation
proposals.motivation: The dominant paradigm in NLP (pre-2026) assumes that scaling model size and data breadth yields universally superior performance. However, for specialized domains like code generation, this leads to immense computational costs and potential inefficiencies, as general models may lack deep, task-specific reasoning. This work challenges that paradigm by rigorously testing whether smaller, domain-specialized models can surpass larger general ones on well-defined coding tasks, with implications for efficient and accessible AI tooling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that smaller language models, when fine-tuned and/or prompted on a tightly clustered set of programming tasks (e.g., secure coding, library-specific APIs, or formal verification), will outperform larger general-purpose LLMs on metrics of correctness, efficiency, and robustness, despite the larger models' superior performance on broad benchmarks.
proposals.method: We will first define a taxonomy of code task clusters (e.g., data structure manipulation, concurrency bugs) using embedding-based clustering on datasets like HumanEval and MBPP. For each cluster, we will fine-tune smaller base models (e.g., CodeLlama 7B) and compare them against large general models (e.g., GPT-4, Claude 3) using a multi-faceted evaluation. This evaluation will combine (1) automated metrics (pass@k), (2) LLM-as-a-judge meta-evaluation via agent debate to assess reasoning quality, and (3) targeted hallucination detection to measure output reliability, extending methods from Neubig's prior work.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, evidence-based framework for evaluating the 'specialization vs. scale' trade-off in code LLMs. It will produce a public benchmark of clustered coding tasks and a methodology for determining when task-specific model development is preferable. The findings will guide more efficient resource allocation in both research and industry.
proposals.novelty: While model specialization is known, this proposal's novelty lies in its systematic, cluster-driven approach combined with a meta-evaluation framework (agent debate, hallucination detection) to dissect *why* and *when* smaller models win, moving beyond simple accuracy comparisons to analyze reasoning verifiability and failure modes.

proposals.title: Beyond GPT for Code: A Structured, Repetition-Aware Neural Architecture for Program Synthesis and Understanding
proposals.motivation: Transformer-based LLMs, while powerful for code, treat it as a linear token sequence, failing to explicitly model its inherent graph structure (e.g., ASTs, data flow) and the high-frequency, localized repetition of identifiers and patterns. This architectural mismatch may limit efficiency, generalization, and the ability to produce verifiably correct code, a critical gap as we push for more reliable AI-assisted programming.
proposals.hypothesis: A neural architecture that explicitly incorporates structural inductive biases for code graphs and a dedicated mechanism for modeling local token repetition will outperform standard decoder-only transformers on code generation and editing tasks, particularly in terms of sample efficiency and factual consistency (e.g., hallucination reduction).
proposals.method: We will design a hybrid encoder-decoder model. The encoder will process a code graph (AST with data-flow edges) using a modified Graph Neural Network (GNN) that aggregates information along syntactic and semantic paths. The decoder will be a structured variant of a transformer, where attention is biased by the encoder's graph-derived node distances and augmented with a 'repetition gate' mechanism—a learned module that explicitly caches and recalls recently generated token patterns within a defined scope, inspired by copy mechanisms but tailored for code's repetitive blocks and variable reuse.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel, open-source architecture benchmarked against state-of-the-art code LLMs, 2) a rigorous meta-evaluation framework (extending agent-debate and fine-grained hallucination detection methods) to assess gains in factual consistency and structural correctness, and 3) insights into which inductive biases are most valuable for code representation.
proposals.novelty: While graph-based code models exist, our integration of a structured-graph encoder with a repetition-aware, gated transformer decoder is novel. The explicit design to capture localized repetition as a first-class architectural feature, rather than relying solely on attention to learn it, is a distinct departure from the GPT paradigm for code.

proposals.title: Meta-Evaluating LLMs as Multi-Lingual Code Evaluators: A Systematic Study Across High- and Low-Resource Programming Languages
proposals.motivation: Current evaluation of Large Language Models (LLMs) on code generation is heavily skewed towards a few high-resource languages like Python and JavaScript, ignoring the vast ecosystem of niche and low-resource programming languages (e.g., Racket, Nim, Elixir). This creates a blind spot in our understanding of LLMs' true capabilities and limitations for software development across diverse ecosystems. A systematic, scalable evaluation framework is needed to assess whether LLM-based evaluators, a promising method for automated assessment, are reliable across this broader linguistic landscape.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the reliability of LLMs as automated evaluators of code generation degrades significantly for low-resource programming languages compared to high-resource ones, and that this degradation can be mitigated by combining agent-debate meta-evaluation with clustering-based language grouping to identify shared failure modes.
proposals.method: We will extend the agent-debate meta-evaluation framework from recent work to a curated dataset spanning 20+ programming languages, selected via clustering based on syntactic, semantic, and corpus-size features. For each language, we will generate code solutions and human annotations for a set of algorithmic tasks. We will then apply the TroVE methodology to induce verifiable 'evaluation toolboxes'—specialized prompts and verification chains—for each language cluster. The core analysis will be a large-scale meta-evaluation, using agent debate to score the accuracy of different LLM-as-judge setups against human judgments, with performance analyzed across language clusters.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first large-scale diagnostic of LLM evaluation quality across the programming language spectrum, identifying specific linguistic features that challenge automated assessment. It will contribute a public benchmark dataset and a set of cluster-specific evaluation toolboxes to the community. The findings will provide concrete guidance for researchers and practitioners on reliably evaluating code generation in underrepresented languages.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied LLM evaluation for code, none have systematically scaled meta-evaluation across dozens of languages or used linguistic clustering to analyze evaluation reliability. The novel integration of agent-debate meta-evaluation with TroVE-induced toolboxes and a linguistically-informed language selection strategy is a significant advance over single-language or small-scale studies.

proposals.title: CodeCausality: A Meta-Evaluation Framework for Disentangling Architectural and Data Factors in Code LLM Performance
proposals.motivation: The rapid scaling of code LLMs has made it difficult to attribute performance gains to specific architectural innovations versus improvements in training data. This lack of causal understanding hinders efficient model development and leads to conflated claims in the literature. A rigorous, disentangled analysis is needed to guide future resource allocation in code model research.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for code generation and understanding, performance on tasks requiring complex reasoning and tool use is more sensitive to specific architectural choices (e.g., modularity, retrieval mechanisms) than to simple data scaling, whereas performance on syntax and idiom completion is primarily driven by the diversity and quality of the pre-training corpus.
proposals.method: We will construct a controlled ablation study using the StarCoder and CodeLlama model families as bases. We will systematically vary architectural components (e.g., attention patterns, parameter-efficient fine-tuning modules) and pre-training data mixtures (e.g., GitHub code, Stack Overflow, synthetic data) in a multi-factor design. Performance will be evaluated across a suite of tasks from the TroVE and HumanEval benchmarks, using agent debate protocols and fine-grained hallucination detection metrics from prior work to ensure robust, verifiable assessment.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first large-scale, causal map linking specific model and data design decisions to performance on distinct code-related capabilities. We will release a public meta-evaluation framework and a set of actionable guidelines for efficient code LLM development. The findings will advance the scientific understanding of what makes code models effective beyond mere scale.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior correlational studies, this proposal employs a controlled experimental design to isolate causal factors. It uniquely integrates rigorous meta-evaluation techniques (agent debate, fine-grained detection) with a systematic ablation of both architecture and data—a combination not yet explored in the code LLM literature.

proposals.title: K-GRASP: Enhancing Code LLMs with Knowledge Graph-Augmented Semantic Programming
proposals.motivation: Current Large Language Models for code (Code LLMs) often generate syntactically valid but semantically flawed programs, such as hallucinating non-existent API calls or violating dataflow constraints. This limits their trustworthiness for complex, programmatic tasks. Integrating structured, verifiable knowledge from Code Property Graphs (CPGs) and API documentation graphs can provide a formal semantic scaffold to ground model generations.
proposals.hypothesis: Augmenting Code LLMs with a retrievable, structured knowledge graph of code semantics (e.g., API specifications, common dataflow patterns) will significantly improve the semantic correctness and tool-using efficiency of generated code, while also enabling more fine-grained, verifiable evaluation of these properties.
proposals.method: We will construct a hybrid knowledge graph from CPGs of open-source repositories and structured API docs (e.g., PyTorch, pandas). A retriever-augmented generation (RAG) architecture will be adapted where a Code LLM (e.g., CodeLlama) queries this graph via a GNN-based retriever to fetch relevant semantic constraints (e.g., function signatures, pre/post-conditions) during code generation. We will evaluate using a meta-evaluation framework inspired by agent debate, where model outputs are checked for consistency against the retrieved knowledge, and on programmatic tasks from benchmarks like TroVE, measuring correctness and efficiency gains.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a novel framework for integrating explicit code semantics into Code LLMs via knowledge graphs, 2) a new benchmark for evaluating semantic hallucination in code generation, and 3) empirical evidence on how structured knowledge impacts both generation quality and the ability to perform scalable, automated meta-evaluation of code.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses graphs for code representation (CPGs) or tool augmentation (TroVE), the integration of a retrievable, general-purpose code semantics knowledge graph into the Code LLM generation loop for real-time constraint enforcement and evaluation is unexplored. This bridges structured program analysis with generative LLMs in a novel, verifiable way.

proposals.title: Data-Efficient Scaling Laws for Code: Can Curation Outperform Corpus Size in LLM Training?
proposals.motivation: Training state-of-the-art code LLMs requires immense, often noisy, web-scraped datasets, raising computational, environmental, and data-quality concerns. Prior scaling laws suggest performance depends primarily on model size and total compute, but this relationship is defined for broad, uncurated data. This work investigates if this paradigm can be broken for the structured domain of code by using high-quality, curated data, potentially reducing training costs and improving model trustworthiness.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for code generation and understanding, a model trained on a significantly smaller but expertly curated and clustered dataset can match or exceed the performance of a model trained on orders-of-magnitude more uncurated data, when controlling for total parameters and compute budget.
proposals.method: We will conduct controlled scaling experiments on the StarCoder/CodeLlama architecture, varying dataset size and quality. We will generate curated datasets by applying fine-grained hallucination detection filters and semantic clustering (inspired by TroVE's toolbox induction) to large raw code corpora like The Stack. We will train models of varying scales (1B to 15B parameters) on these curated sets and on progressively larger random subsets of raw data. Performance will be evaluated across diverse programming tasks (e.g., HumanEval, MBPP) using rigorous meta-evaluation via agent debate to ensure reliable metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce new, domain-specific scaling laws for code LLMs that account for data quality, offering a practical roadmap for more sustainable and effective model training. It will also deliver a novel methodology for creating high-value code training corpora through clustering and verification, advancing data-centric AI for programming languages.
proposals.novelty: While data curation is known to help, we systematically test if it can fundamentally alter scaling laws, challenging the 'more data is always better' axiom. The integration of verifiable task clustering (Trove) with scalable meta-evaluation (Agent Debate) for this investigation is a novel methodological synthesis.

proposals.title: Meta-Aligned Tiered Evaluation (MATE): A Framework for Comparable and Diagnostically Rich Cross-Lingual LLM Assessment
proposals.motivation: Current evaluation of large language models (LLMs) across languages is fragmented, with a proliferation of language-specific benchmarks that hinder direct comparability and high-level progress tracking. This fragmentation, coupled with known issues of LLM-as-a-judge bias and metric gaming, makes it difficult to determine if improvements on a specific benchmark reflect genuine, generalizable capability gains or are artifacts of the evaluation setup. A unified yet nuanced evaluation framework is urgently needed to guide the responsible and equitable development of multilingual AI.
proposals.hypothesis: A two-tiered evaluation system, where a small set of cross-lingual foundation metrics is continuously meta-aligned with a suite of granular diagnostic evaluations, can provide both robust comparability across languages and detailed, actionable performance insights, mitigating the risks of metric fragmentation and gaming.
proposals.method: We will first define a minimal set of foundation metrics (e.g., for reasoning, instruction following, factuality) using highly standardized, automatically translatable formats (inspired by the programmatic structure of TroVE). Second, we will curate and generate modular diagnostic suites for a diverse set of languages and tasks. The core innovation is the meta-evaluation layer, which will employ scalable, automated 'agent debate' protocols (extending Neubig's prior work on meta-evaluation) to continuously measure the correlation and causal relationships between diagnostic scores and foundation metrics. This will involve training lightweight 'alignment probes' to predict foundation performance from diagnostic outputs and identify adversarial divergences.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a practical, open-source framework for comparable multilingual LLM evaluation, 2) new insights into the alignment (or misalignment) between detailed diagnostics and high-level capability measures across languages, and 3) a methodology for the continuous audit and stabilization of evaluation ecosystems against fragmentation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has proposed meta-evaluation or multi-tiered benchmarks in isolation, this integrates them into a closed-loop system specifically designed for the cross-lingual setting, using automated agent debate and alignment probing to actively enforce comparability across diverse diagnostic modules.

proposals.title: Beyond Aggregate Scores: A Framework for Fine-Grained, Language-Specific Evaluation of LLMs
proposals.motivation: Current LLM evaluation is dominated by aggregate benchmark scores (e.g., MMLU, BIG-bench), which obscure critical, language-specific failure modes and provide little actionable insight for model improvement. This is especially problematic for multilingual and code generation tasks, where nuanced errors in grammar, semantics, or functional correctness are lost in a single-number summary. A shift towards diagnostic, fine-grained evaluation is needed to understand model capabilities and limitations across the full spectrum of languages and tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that decomposing aggregate evaluation scores into a structured set of language- and task-specific error categories will reveal systematic, previously hidden failure patterns in LLMs, enabling more targeted model debugging and development.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework that first uses clustering techniques (inspired by Neubig's prior work) on model outputs across diverse languages and programming tasks to inductively identify common error types. For each error cluster, we will design automated, verifiable metrics (extending ideas from TroVE) to quantify its prevalence. Finally, we will employ meta-evaluation via agent debate (as in 'Can LLMs be Trusted for Evaluation?') to validate the correlation of these automated metrics with fine-grained human judgments of error severity and type.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a public framework and benchmark for fine-grained, multilingual LLM evaluation, 2) a taxonomy of error types specific to low-resource languages and code generation, and 3) empirical analysis revealing how aggregate scores mask critical weaknesses, guiding future model development towards more robust, equitable performance.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has focused on either aggregate metrics or monolingual hallucination detection, our approach uniquely integrates multilingual error clustering with verifiable, programmatic metric generation and LLM-based meta-evaluation to create a comprehensive, diagnostic evaluation system.

proposals.title: The Multilingual Model Observatory: A Standardized, Automated Benchmarking Service for Longitudinal Evaluation
proposals.motivation: The rapid release of new multilingual models (e.g., mBERT, XLM-R, mT5) has led to a crisis of inconsistent evaluation. Comparisons are often ad-hoc, using different task subsets, data splits, or evaluation frameworks, making it difficult to track true progress or attribute gains fairly. This mirrors the pre-standardization era in astronomy, where each telescope used its own survey, hindering meta-analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: Establishing a permanent, automated benchmarking observatory with a fixed, core evaluation battery will produce a consistent longitudinal dataset that enables more reliable tracking of progress, clearer identification of capability trends, and fairer comparisons across models and time.
proposals.method: We will design and deploy an automated service that, upon submission of a model identifier (e.g., from Hugging Face), runs a standardized suite of evaluations. The core battery will include a curated, fixed subset of tasks from benchmarks like XTREME and XGLUE, plus diagnostic probes for linguistic phenomena. Inspired by TroVE's programmatic task framework, evaluations will be defined as verifiable, version-controlled pipelines. All results, model metadata, and timestamps will be archived in a public repository, with analysis tools for clustering model capabilities and tracking trends over time.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the community with 1) a canonical, version-controlled dataset of model capabilities over time, 2) an automated service for consistent evaluation, reducing redundant effort, and 3) tools for meta-evaluation and trend analysis, directly extending Neubig's work on scalable meta-evaluation and hallucination detection to the multilingual domain.
proposals.novelty: While many benchmarks exist, the novelty lies in the *observatory* concept: a permanent, automated service for systematic longitudinal tracking, analogous to astronomical sky surveys, rather than a static benchmark. This shifts the focus from one-off leaderboards to studying the evolution of model families.

proposals.title: Prime-Boost Meta-Learning for Efficient and Stable Few-Shot Cross-Lingual Adaptation
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) exhibit significant performance disparities across languages, especially for low-resource ones. While meta-learning offers a promising framework for few-shot adaptation, existing methods often struggle with instability and inefficiency when rapidly adapting to novel, linguistically distant languages. A principled approach to structure the meta-learning process for robust cross-lingual generalization is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: Explicitly structuring meta-learning into a biologically-inspired prime-boost paradigm—separating the acquisition of universal adaptation algorithms from their rapid, targeted deployment—will yield more stable, sample-efficient, and effective few-shot adaptation to new languages compared to standard meta-learning or fine-tuning baselines.
proposals.method: We will implement a two-phase MAML (Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning) variant. The 'priming' phase performs meta-pre-training across a diverse set of medium- and high-resource languages, learning a robust initialization and adaptation rule. The novel 'boosting' phase then meta-adapts this primed model specifically for a held-out target language: we will meta-learn a secondary, lightweight optimizer or a set of language-specific scaling factors that modulate the primed adaptation rule, using only the few-shot support set. This process will be evaluated on diverse NLP tasks (e.g., NLI, tagging) across language clusters.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel meta-learning algorithm for cross-lingual transfer that explicitly decouples general adaptation skill learning from rapid specialization. 2) Empirical evidence on the stability and efficiency gains of this structured approach for low-resource languages. 3) A new framework for biologically-inspired learning strategies in NLP.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the structured analogy from immunology, translating the prime-boost vaccination strategy into a concrete, two-phase meta-learning algorithm designed explicitly for the linguistic domain, moving beyond a simple application of existing meta-learning.

ideas.title: Reinforcement Learning for Adaptive Query Formulation and Retrieval Policy
ideas.description: This research proposes a unified reinforcement learning framework where the agent's action space encompasses both the decision to retrieve and the method for generating the retrieval query. The agent learns a policy that selects from a repertoire of query formulation techniques—such as query prediction, question generation, or claim-based extraction—based on the current dialogue or document context and the estimated utility of external information. This enables the system to dynamically choose whether to retrieve and, if so, to craft the most effective query type for that moment, optimizing for downstream task performance rather than a static retrieval strategy.

ideas.title: Synthetic Data Augmentation via Adversarial Curriculum Learning for Retrieval
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computational immunology, where synthetic epitopes are generated to ensure vaccine coverage against future viral variants. The analogous problem is ensuring a retrieval corpus has sufficient coverage for future, unseen queries in an active generation task. The adapted solution is to create an adversarial curriculum learning framework. A generator model produces synthetic documents aimed at the current retriever's failure modes or low-density areas in the query embedding space. These synthetic documents are added to the corpus, and the retriever is fine-tuned. The improved retriever's new weaknesses are then identified, guiding the next, more difficult round of targeted synthetic generation. This creates a scaling dual process where both the generator (creating harder synthetic data) and the retriever (learning from it) improve iteratively, systematically expanding the corpus's effective coverage for the target task.

ideas.title: Calibration for open-ended generation

ideas.title: Bayesian Model Averaging for Retrieval Uncertainty
ideas.description: This idea adapts the ensemble forecasting method from climate science to improve uncertainty estimation for retrieval in language models. In weather prediction, an ensemble of models provides a distribution of forecasts, and their spread is a trusted measure of uncertainty. Adapted here, we propose using a diverse ensemble of language models (varying in architecture, size, or training) rather than a single model. The system would compute a robust uncertainty signal—such as the variance or entropy across the ensemble's output distributions for a given prompt—and use this aggregated measure, rather than a single model's token-level confidence, to decide when to trigger external retrieval. This approach captures deeper epistemic uncertainty about the correct generation path, making retrieval activation more reliable and less prone to the overconfidence of any single model.

ideas.title: Tiered Evaluation with Meta-Alignment for Cross-Lingual Comparability
ideas.description: This framework proposes a two-tiered evaluation system to balance specificity with comparability. The first tier consists of a small, carefully designed set of foundation metrics that are applicable and standardized across all languages and a broad set of tasks, enabling high-level progress tracking. The second tier incorporates modular, language- and task-specific diagnostic evaluations that provide detailed, nuanced performance insights. A critical meta-evaluation component continuously measures the correlation and divergence between the diagnostic modules and the foundation metrics, ensuring that gains in specific evaluations reflect genuine, comparable improvements rather than metric gaming or fragmentation.

ideas.title: Adversarially Grounded Indexing (AGI): Multi-Agent Scrutiny for Retrieval Structure
ideas.description: This system prevents the amplification of a single LM's flaws by using an adversarial framework to guide retrieval indexing. A generator model proposes indexing strategies based on its generation patterns, but these proposals are rigorously critiqued by a separate, independent model or validation suite that checks for hallucinations, contradictions with the knowledge base, and biases. The indexing is dynamically updated only with proposals that pass this adversarial filter, ensuring the retrieval structure becomes more robust and factually anchored over time. This turns the generation process into a source of hypotheses about knowledge organization, which are then stress-tested before being embedded into the system's infrastructure.

ideas.title: Cross-Lingual Model Adaptation via Immune System-Inspired Meta-Learning
ideas.description: This idea applies an analogy from computational immunology and vaccine design to solve the problem of few-shot cross-lingual adaptation. The analogous solution is the 'prime-boost' vaccination strategy, which trains the adaptive immune system for broad recognition and rapid, targeted response to new variants. Adapted to meta-learning, the model undergoes a two-phase process: 1) A 'priming' meta-pre-training phase across many languages to internalize robust, general-purpose adaptation algorithms, and 2) A 'boosting' few-shot meta-adaptation phase for a new target language, where the meta-learner's update rule is specifically optimized to rapidly specialize the primed adaptation mechanisms using the novel language's minimal data. This biologically-inspired structure aims to achieve more efficient and stable few-shot learning across languages by explicitly separating the learning of universal adaptation skills from their swift deployment to new linguistic environments.

ideas.title: Optimal Granularity Search via Multi-Scale Information Theory
ideas.description: This research idea challenges the assumption that sentence-level prediction is the optimal query unit by drawing an analogy from computational neuroscience. In sensory processing, identifying the optimal spatial/temporal scale (receptive field) for information encoding is a solved problem using multi-scale information theory. We adapt this solution by treating different linguistic granularities (words, phrases, sentences, paragraphs, concepts) as different 'receptive fields' for a language model's query generation mechanism. The core investigation involves designing experiments to measure a task-specific information-theoretic metric (e.g., mutual information between the query unit and a target future output) across these scales. This will determine if an optimal granularity exists, whether it aligns with the sentence level, and how it varies with task objectives (e.g., fact retrieval vs. narrative continuation), thereby providing a principled, empirical basis for selecting query units in forward-looking generation systems.

ideas.title: Retrieval-Augmented Stylistic Transfer via Biomimetic Pattern Recognition
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that retrieval is only for factual knowledge by proposing its use to augment style, tone, and narrative structure. Drawing an analogy from computational biology and protein design—where databases are searched for abstract structural motifs to engineer novel proteins—we adapt this solution to NLP. The adapted idea is to build a retrieval system that indexes text based on abstract stylistic and narrative patterns rather than factual content. Given a desired stylistic effect (e.g., 'conversational tone', 'non-linear narrative'), the system retrieves exemplar text segments demonstrating that pattern. A language model then analyzes these exemplars to extract the underlying abstract pattern or 'template', which is subsequently applied to guide the generation or transformation of new content, enabling precise, retrieval-augmented control over stylistic and structural elements of text.

ideas.title: Confidence-Triggered Verification via Predictive Coding
ideas.description: This idea inverts the standard logic of using low confidence to trigger retrieval by drawing an analogy from computational neuroscience's predictive processing frameworks. In predictive coding, high confidence (a close match between prediction and input) often triggers increased precision weighting on prediction errors to prevent perceptual oversights. The analogous solution is adapted by designing an AI system where a high-confidence output from a language model does not terminate reasoning but instead activates a dedicated verification module. This module uses the high-confidence claim as a precise query to perform targeted retrieval from external knowledge sources, specifically looking for disconfirming evidence or subtle anomalies, thereby using confidence as a direct indicator for the need to verify and challenge the system's own assumptions.

ideas.title: Establish a Standardized Benchmarking Observatory for Multilingual Models
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from astronomy's use of standardized sky surveys, this idea adapts the solution of creating a permanent observatory for systematic cataloging. In astronomy, projects like the Sloan Digital Sky Survey provide a consistent framework for evaluating new telescopes and instruments. Adapted to NLP, the research idea is to establish a Standardized Benchmarking Observatory for Multilingual Models. This platform would function as an automated, version-controlled service that runs a core, fixed battery of evaluations (e.g., a defined subset of XTREME tasks plus essential diagnostic probes) on every newly released multilingual encoder. All results, along with model identifiers and evaluation timestamps, would be archived in a central repository. This solves the analogous problem of inconsistent, one-off comparisons by providing a consistent longitudinal dataset, enabling the research community to systematically track progress, analyze trends, and fairly compare the capabilities of models developed across different times and institutions.

ideas.title: Amortized Information-Gain Prediction for Low-Latency Retrieval Triggering
ideas.description: This system trains a small neural network to predict the potential information gain of retrieval without performing expensive online calculations. The predictor is trained offline on a corpus of generation episodes where true information-theoretic metrics are computed, using efficient contextual features from the current generation step as input. During real-time inference, this fast proxy model evaluates whether to trigger a retrieval, balancing accuracy with minimal added latency. This approach makes information-theoretic retrieval triggering practical for real-world applications.

ideas.title: Progressive Overload Training for Neural Retrieval
ideas.description: This idea adapts the principle of progressive overload from sports science to improve a model's active retrieval capability. In strength training, athletes systematically increase training stress—through heavier weights or more complex movements—to force physiological adaptation while avoiding injury or plateaus. Similarly, for retrieval training, we design a curriculum that starts with simple retrieval tasks from small, highly relevant corpora with clear retrieval cues. The difficulty is then progressively increased by expanding the corpus size, introducing semantic distractors, adding noise to the retrieval signal, and eventually requiring complex multi-hop reasoning. This structured, escalating challenge forces the model's retrieval mechanisms to adapt and strengthen gradually, leading to more robust and capable retrieval performance than training on uniformly difficult tasks from the outset.

ideas.title: Adaptive Sensing for Generative Agents: Extending FLARE with a Control-Theoretic Trigger
ideas.description: This research seed proposes extending the FLARE (Forward-Looking Active REtrieval) method beyond long-form knowledge generation to diverse tasks like dialogue and code generation by drawing an analogy from control systems engineering. The analogous problem is efficient sensing in resource-constrained systems, solved via adaptive, event-triggered sensing that activates measurements only when a monitored condition warrants it. The adapted idea is to develop a framework where the retrieval action is triggered not by a generic schedule, but by a dynamically evaluated, task-specific condition—such as dialogue topic shift or code complexity uncertainty—effectively creating a feedback control loop for information gathering tailored to the generative task's unique demands and constraints.

ideas.title: Consistency-Guided Retrieval via Multi-Hypothesis Uncertainty
ideas.description: This approach replaces token-level confidence with a robust uncertainty measure derived from the model's own generative consistency across multiple sampling runs. The system generates several candidate continuations for ambiguous queries and computes their semantic variance. When high-likelihood generations diverge significantly in meaning or factual content, it indicates genuine parametric uncertainty, triggering retrieval. This method directly ties retrieval decisions to the model's actual knowledge gaps rather than relying on poorly calibrated confidence scores, creating a self-aligned uncertainty detection mechanism.

ideas.title: Curriculum Learning for Uncertainty-Aware Retrieval (CURE)
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a curriculum learning framework where the difficulty of retrieval decisions is progressively increased, but the core training signal for advancing through the curriculum is the model's own evolving, sophisticated confidence estimates. Initially, the model is trained on simple tasks where clear uncertainty metrics (e.g., predictive entropy, ensemble variance) can reliably indicate the need for retrieval. As training progresses, the complexity of examples increases, forcing the model to refine its confidence estimators to handle ambiguous or compositional knowledge gaps. This co-evolution of retrieval policy and uncertainty calibration results in a system that not only learns when to retrieve more effectively but also develops a more nuanced and reliable internal representation of its own limitations.

ideas.title: MCTS-Guided Long-Horizon Retrieval for Coherent Book-Length Generation
ideas.description: This research direction combines the challenge of generating coherent, book-length narratives from massive document corpora with Monte Carlo Tree Search for strategic planning. The core innovation is using MCTS to simulate multiple high-level narrative trajectories, evaluating them not just for local quality but for their long-term feasibility given the constraints and opportunities of the retrieval corpus. This allows the system to make informed, anticipatory retrieval decisions—seeding the context with foundational concepts early and planning for pivotal reveals or thematic payoffs much later in the text. The result is a generation system that can strategically navigate a vast information space to construct a globally planned, coherent long-form narrative, overcoming the myopia typical of autoregressive models.

ideas.title: Verified Synthetic Corpus Expansion with Multi-Fidelity Filtering
ideas.description: This system generates candidate documents for retrieval augmentation but subjects them to a stringent verification pipeline before adding them to the corpus. Each candidate is scored on dimensions of factual accuracy, stylistic authenticity, and logical consistency against trusted references and the existing corpus distribution. Only candidates surpassing adaptive thresholds on all dimensions are integrated, ensuring the expanded corpus maintains high quality. This closed-loop, generate-verify-augment process improves coverage while actively safeguarding against corpus pollution from poor generations.

ideas.title: Information-Theoretic Intrinsic Rewards for RL-Based Retrieval Policy
ideas.description: This research direction combines reinforcement learning (RL) for sequential retrieval decisions with information theory to shape the agent's learning objective. The RL agent's policy decides when and what to retrieve, but its reward function is intrinsically defined by information-theoretic measures like the reduction in entropy or the mutual information between the retrieval result and the ongoing generation. This allows the agent to learn not just to retrieve for immediate correctness, but to strategically retrieve information that maximally reduces uncertainty about the task. Consequently, the system develops a proactive retrieval strategy that balances the cost of retrieval against the expected information gain, leading to more efficient and informative interactions with knowledge sources.

ideas.title: Adapt Protein Folding Path Planning to LLM Generation Planning
ideas.description: This idea adapts solution strategies from computational protein folding to the problem of planning retrieval-augmented generation paths for an LLM. In protein folding, researchers model the energy landscape as a network of metastable states and use path-sampling algorithms to find efficient, low-energy pathways to the folded structure, avoiding brute-force search. Analogously, we can model the space of potential LLM outputs as a landscape of 'generation states' defined by semantic content, partial arguments, or incorporated knowledge snippets. A Monte Carlo Tree Search planner is then used to simulate transitions between these states—via steps of token generation or knowledge retrieval—evaluating each potential path with a reward function based on overall coherence, factual accuracy, and goal alignment. This allows the system to plan a sequence of reasoning and retrieval actions that maximizes final output quality, efficiently navigating the combinatorial space of possible generation trajectories.

ideas.title: Temporally Coherent Generation via Transactional Knowledge Snapshots
ideas.description: This system guarantees the temporal coherence of generated text by treating each generation task as a transaction against a versioned, dynamic knowledge base. At task initiation, it captures a consistent snapshot of the relevant knowledge, ensuring all retrievals for that output are from an immutable state. Knowledge updates are processed asynchronously, applied only between discrete generation tasks. An optional validation layer proactively detects and surfaces potential factual drifts or contradictions introduced by updates, allowing for controlled integration and maintaining output reliability.

ideas.title: Use active learning for prompt selection

ideas.title: Dynamic Knowledge Integration via a Write-Ahead Log
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a static external knowledge base is sufficient for generation by proposing a dynamic, updatable source. Drawing an analogy from Database Management Systems, specifically the Write-Ahead Log (WAL) mechanism used for durability and replication, we adapt this solution. The core research idea is to implement a similar logging system for knowledge updates: a primary knowledge source makes changes, which are first recorded in a persistent, sequential log. During text generation, the language model's retrieval mechanism or a dedicated lightweight module queries both its static parameters and this real-time update log. This allows the model to integrate new facts, correct errors, and reflect evolving world states dynamically, mimicking how a database replica stays current by consuming a transaction log, thereby solving the problem of temporal grounding and knowledge staleness without constant model retraining.

ideas.title: Adapting Ant Colony Optimization for Probabilistic Query Formulation
ideas.description: This idea adapts the problem-solving approach from Ant Colony Optimization in swarm intelligence to the challenge of generating forward-looking queries. The analogous problem—finding optimal paths in a graph—is solved by having multiple agents (ants) build candidate solutions probabilistically, using a shared pheromone map to reinforce successful components. Adapted to query formulation, the research would investigate techniques to generate not one but a probabilistic swarm of alternative queries. A model would learn and maintain dynamic weights ('pheromone trails') linking various query elements to historical success metrics. Over iterations, the system would explore diverse formulations while exploiting and reinforcing the components of queries that prove most effective for the task, moving beyond simple next-sentence prediction to a population-based, reinforcement-guided search for optimal query construction.

ideas.title: Controlled Stylistic Retrieval with Coherence-Guided Fusion
ideas.description: This system actively retrieves exemplars for style, tone, and narrative structure but governs the process with an explicit coherence model. It first decomposes the generation task into a target stylistic profile, then retrieves a diverse set of candidate passages. A learned discriminator, trained to predict stylistic harmony and contextual fit, filters and weights these candidates. The final generation integrates these guided stylistic signals to produce output that is both creatively augmented and contextually consistent, overcoming the pitfalls of naive stylistic blending.

ideas.title: Learned Retrieval Gating for Robust Active RAG on Edge Devices
ideas.description: This system introduces a dedicated, optimized gating module that decides when to trigger retrievals in an active RAG pipeline for edge deployment. The gating classifier analyzes the input query and the small LM's preliminary processing to estimate knowledge gaps, preventing unnecessary retrievals for simple facts and ensuring critical retrievals for complex questions. By offloading and specializing the retrieval decision logic, the primary small LM can focus solely on synthesis, improving both system efficiency and answer robustness. The gating policy is co-designed with the hardware constraints, allowing dynamic adjustment of the retrieval threshold based on available power and latency budgets.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Retrieval Architecture for Book-Length Narrative Generation
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from computational genomics, specifically genome assembly algorithms, to the problem of scaling retrieval-augmented generation to book-length narratives. In genomics, overlap-layout-consensus and de Bruijn graph methods reconstruct entire chromosomes from billions of short DNA reads by breaking sequences into k-mers, building overlap graphs to find connections, and using hierarchical scaffolding to resolve large-scale structure. Adapted to narrative generation, the corpus is processed into narrative 'k-mers' (semantic text units). A hierarchical retrieval architecture is then employed: a local tier retrieves for sentence-to-paragraph coherence using fine-grained overlaps, while a global tier manages chapter-to-plot arc consistency through a scaffold of key narrative nodes. This dual-scale approach, mirroring genomic assembly's local assembly and global scaffolding, enables the system to manage retrieval across massive corpora and maintain long-range coherence, thereby generating structured, book-length narratives from a vast, fragmented knowledge base.

ideas.title: Co-Adaptive Tuning for RAG using a Shared Lightweight Control Network
ideas.description: This idea proposes a method for joint, parameter-efficient fine-tuning of LM and retrieval components in active RAG, inspired by co-adaptive algorithms in Brain-Computer Interfaces. In BCIs, users and decoder models co-adapt through a shared, low-dimensional control interface for efficient synergy. Adapted to RAG, we introduce a small, trainable 'control network' that interfaces with both the frozen base LM and the frozen retriever. During fine-tuning, only this shared network and its lightweight connection parameters are updated. The unified gradient from the end-task loss jointly optimizes how the LM attends to and uses retrieved information and how the retriever filters and ranks candidate passages, ensuring both components evolve synergistically towards the task objective with minimal parameter overhead, preventing catastrophic forgetting and promoting stable convergence.

ideas.title: Generative Feedback for Dynamic Index Tuning
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from adaptive radar signal processing, this idea proposes using the LM's generation patterns as a feedback signal to dynamically tune retrieval indexing. In radar, the system adapts its scanning parameters based on reflections of its own transmissions. Similarly, here, the LM's generative outputs—its confidence distributions, hallucination signatures, and uncertainty markers—are continuously analyzed. This analysis then informs real-time adjustments to how the external knowledge base is indexed or structured, such as by creating denser vector clusters around topics of low generative confidence or re-weighting document relevance based on the model's inferred knowledge gaps. This creates a closed-loop, dual system where generation guides retrieval structuring, which in turn improves future generation, scaling the system's capability through self-informed optimization.

ideas.title: Meta-Learned Online Bayesian Optimization for Adaptive Hyperparameter Tuning
ideas.description: This system addresses the non-stationarity of real-world tasks by combining meta-learning with online Bayesian optimization. A meta-model is first pre-trained on a broad spectrum of tasks to learn generalizable priors over effective hyperparameter configurations. During deployment, a lightweight online Bayesian optimization loop uses these priors for rapid cold-start and continuously adjusts hyperparameters like confidence thresholds based on streaming performance feedback. This enables the system to maintain efficiency and robustness against concept drift, task shifts, and changing operational constraints without manual intervention.

ideas.title: Adapt Pharmacokinetic Modeling for Adaptive Bayesian Hyperparameter Tuning
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from clinical pharmacology, where Bayesian pharmacokinetic modeling is used for adaptive, personalized drug dosing, to the problem of tuning hyperparameters like confidence thresholds and retrieval intervals in machine learning. The analogous solution involves building a prior model from population data (meta-learning on past tasks) and then using Bayesian inference to efficiently update hyperparameters for a specific new task based on sparse performance feedback, analogous to adjusting a drug dose based on periodic blood tests. The adapted research idea is to frame each new task as a 'patient' requiring a personalized 'treatment regimen' of hyperparameters. A Bayesian optimization framework will be designed to sequentially query the task with proposed hyperparameter settings, observe the resulting performance metric, and update a probabilistic model to rapidly converge on the optimal, task-specific settings, thereby efficiently maintaining performance in an optimal target zone.

ideas.title: Optimal Stopping in Document Retrieval: An Information-Theoretic Filter
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that retrieving more documents always improves factual accuracy by drawing an analogy from signal processing, where optimal stopping rules prevent noise accumulation. The analogous solution involves dynamically assessing incoming data against a signal model to stop acquisition when noise outweighs benefit. Adapted to retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), the research would develop a framework where the retrieval process actively evaluates the marginal information utility of each new document, stopping once a predicted noise-introduction threshold is crossed, thereby studying and mitigating the conditions under which extra retrieval harms correctness rather than helps it.

A symbolic planner maintains constraints on knowledge graph traversal and context window limits to ensure feasible rollouts. The proxy model is continuously fine-tuned on a small fraction of actual LLM outputs, keeping its value predictions aligned with the true generative process. This enables deep, real-time planning over potential retrieval paths without requiring expensive full LLM forward passes during search.

ideas.title: Meta-Cognitive Uncertainty Triggers for Selective Verification
ideas.description: This system moves beyond using final-output confidence as a retrieval trigger. Instead, it continuously monitors the model's own reasoning process for signs of meta-cognitive uncertainty, such as high variance in latent representations during generation or internal contradiction between supporting rationales. A retrieval from external knowledge bases is initiated when the model displays high confidence in its final answer but low confidence in the coherence of the path it took to get there. This approach specifically targets the 'confidently wrong' failure mode by forcing verification when the model's internal state signals potential incoherence, even if the final output is delivered with high certainty.

ideas.title: Adaptive Satellite Downlink Scheduling for Edge RAG Systems
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from satellite communication systems where limited-power satellites use adaptive downlink scheduling to optimize data transmission, this research adapts that solution to edge RAG deployment. The core idea is to implement active RAG with small LMs using an adaptive retrieval scheduler that dynamically adjusts retrieval frequency and depth based on real-time resource constraints (battery, compute, bandwidth) and query context. Rather than using fixed retrieval intervals, the system employs a lightweight predictive model to determine optimal retrieval timing and granularity, significantly reducing computational and energy overhead while maintaining response quality suitable for edge environments.
